title: Conditional Flow Matching for Probabilistic Downscaling of Maximum 3-day Snowfall in Alaska
summary: Precipitation in complex terrain is governed by orographic processes operating at scales of a few kilometers, yet climate models typically run at resolutions of 50--100~km where this topographic detail is absent. Dynamical downscaling with high-resolution regional models such as WRF can resolve these processes, but the computational cost -- months of wall-clock time per scenario -- precludes the large ensembles needed for uncertainty quantification. We present WxFlow, a conditional generative model based on flow matching that learns to map coarse-resolution climate model output and high-resolution topography to calibrated probabilistic ensembles of fine-scale precipitation fields. Applied to 4~km WRF simulations of maximum 3-day snowfall over southeast Alaska, WxFlow achieves 87.8\% improvement in spectral fidelity and dramatically lower Continuous Ranked Probability Scores relative to conventional lapse-rate-corrected bicubic downscaling, while generating 50-member ensembles in seconds on a laptop. Ensemble spread is spatially coherent and governed by topography, reflecting physically plausible uncertainty structure. All code is available at https://github.com/glide-ism/wrf-flow.

AI.tldr: WxFlow is a conditional generative model using flow matching that efficiently produces calibrated probabilistic ensembles of fine-scale precipitation fields from coarse climate model data and high-resolution topography, achieving near-perfect spectral fidelity and massive speed improvements over traditional methods.
AI.motivation: Dynamical downscaling with high-resolution regional models (e.g., WRF) is computationally prohibitive for large ensembles needed for uncertainty quantification in complex terrain precipitation.
AI.method: A conditional generative model based on flow matching (WxFlow) that maps coarse climate model output and high-resolution topography to probabilistic ensembles of fine-scale precipitation fields.
AI.result: 87.8% improvement in spectral fidelity, dramatically lower CRPS compared to lapse-rate-corrected bicubic downscaling, generates 50-member ensembles in seconds on a laptop with physically plausible uncertainty structure.
AI.conclusion: WxFlow enables fast, calibrated probabilistic downscaling for precipitation in complex terrain, making large ensemble uncertainty quantification feasible without expensive dynamical models.

title: Deterministic Realization of Classical Dissipation on Quantum Computers
summary: Lattice Boltzmann (LB) on quantum devices must reconcile unitary gate evolution with the dissipative \emph{collision} step. In the multiple-relaxation-time (MRT) class, we work in the common setting of \emph{modewise diagonal} moment relaxation, $δm_r'=λ_r\,δm_r$ with $λ_r\in[-1,1]$ (overrelaxation if $λ_r<0$). Embedding that contraction in a unitary by block encoding or a linear combination of unitaries (LCU) typically yields subunitary success probability that decays multiplicatively across modes, sites, and time, a key bottleneck for quantum LB. \emph{For the dissipative MRT block alone} we give a \emph{block-encoding-free} construction: a signed \emph{two-rail} population encoding, then a completely positive trace-preserving (CPTP) map (per-rail amplitude damping with survival $|λ_r|$ and, if $λ_r<0$, a rail SWAP) so that, after the decode, the map agrees with classical MRT relaxation exactly (expectations of the rail number operators, common encoding--decode scale). Trace preservation gives success probability $1$ for that substage. The main result is the dissipative MRT block; construction of the equilibrium moment vector~$m^{\mathrm{eq}}=Mf^{\mathrm{eq}}$ (prescribed~$f^{\mathrm{eq}}$, host moment matrix~$M$; notation as in Section~\ref{subsec:generic-mrt}), moment transforms, streaming, and boundaries are composed with it as in a standard host pipeline and lie outside the scope of the formal theorem. Hybrid and fully coherent encodings, adaptive scales, Carleman-based context, and a one-rail no-go in the same nonnegative population framework are in the main text. Audits of the open-channel map on a long LBM collide-stream simulation and on stencil-free inputs both match the target to machine precision.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a block-encoding-free, completely positive trace-preserving (CPTP) map for the dissipative MRT collision step in quantum Lattice Boltzmann methods, achieving unit success probability using a signed two-rail population encoding and per-rail amplitude damping with rail SWAP for overrelaxation.
AI.motivation: Existing quantum LB approaches suffer from subunitary success probability that decays multiplicatively across modes, sites, and time due to block encoding or LCU embeddings of the dissipative contraction, creating a key bottleneck for practical quantum Lattice Boltzmann simulations.
AI.method: A signed two-rail population encoding is introduced, followed by a CPTP map consisting of per-rail amplitude damping (with survival probability |λᵣ|) and, when λᵣ < 0 (overrelaxation), a rail SWAP operation. After decoding, this exactly reproduces the classical MRT relaxation in expectation of the rail number operators.
AI.result: The dissipative MRT block achieves trace preservation (success probability = 1) for the substage, and audits on long LBM collide-stream simulations and stencil-free inputs match the target to machine precision.
AI.conclusion: This construction removes the multiplicative decay bottleneck for the dissipative MRT block, though moment transforms, streaming, boundaries, and equilibrium moment construction remain part of the standard host pipeline outside the formal theorem's scope.

title: Basic linear algebra methods for quantum problems
summary: Making new methods for quantum problems often relies on using basic operations in linear algebra. Often these routines are hidden behind well-known libraries that have been optimized over decades. Attempting to improve on those basic routines would be highly time-consuming. We aim in this article to review those basic routines and provide a knowledge foundation for how to perform basic operations on a computer that would be inaccessible with pen and paper. Elementary details on the solutions to linear algebra problems and computational complexity are reviewed. The focus is on solving eigenvalue problems for quantum systems, but the discussion is generic to many other applications. Common matrix forms relevant to quantum systems and their solution strategies are covered. The discussion extends to computational numerical methods for which the most efficient functions exist in freely available libraries. These include eigenvalue, Schur, QR, LU, LDL, Cholesky, and singular value decompositions. The algorithms for obtaining some of these decompositions are discussed, with focus being placed on those used in modern libraries.

AI.tldr: This paper reviews fundamental linear algebra routines and computational methods used in quantum problems, covering eigenvalue problems, matrix decompositions (QR, LU, LDL, Cholesky, SVD, Schur), and their implementations in optimized open-source libraries.
AI.motivation: Basic linear algebra operations are essential for quantum problem-solving, yet these routines are often hidden behind optimized libraries. Understanding them is necessary for developing new quantum methods, as manual calculations are impractical for complex systems.
AI.method: The paper provides a comprehensive review of elementary linear algebra solutions and computational complexity, focusing on eigenvalue problems and common matrix forms relevant to quantum systems. It discusses algorithms for obtaining various matrix decompositions as implemented in modern freely available libraries.
AI.result: A knowledge foundation covering key decompositions: eigenvalue, Schur, QR, LU, LDL, Cholesky, and singular value decompositions, with emphasis on their relevance to quantum systems and efficient implementation strategies.
AI.conclusion: Understanding the underlying linear algebra routines is crucial for advancing quantum computational methods, and optimized open-source libraries provide efficient implementations that would be inaccessible through manual computation.

title: Comparative Study of Bending Analysis using Physics-Informed Neural Networks and Numerical Dynamic Deflection in Perforated nanobeam
summary: In this chapter, we investigate the bending behavior of a perforated nanobeam subjected to sinusoidal loading using an efficient and computationally robust Physics-Informed Functional Link Constrained Framework with Domain Mapping (DFL-TFC) method. Our aim is to determine the relationship between static bending response and dynamic deflection of a perforated nanobeam for various perforation cases. The static bending is obtained using the FL-TFC with Domain mapped method, whereas dynamic deflection is determined using the Galerkin method. The proposed approach employs the theory of functional connections (TFC) to systematically embed governing differential equation constraints into a constrained expression (CE), which exactly satisfies all prescribed initial and boundary conditions (ICs and BCs) and domain of differential equation is mapped to domain of orthogonal polynomials. Within this framework, the free function appearing in the constrained expression is expressed through a functional link neural network (FLNN). The cost is minimized by the mean square residual of DE, allowing training without requiring complex deep network architectures. Relationship between static and dynamic defection of simply-supported (S-S) perforated nanobeams has been investigated here. FL-TFC with Domain mapped method eliminates the need for deep and complex neural network architectures while ensuring accuracy, efficiency, and strict satisfaction of boundary conditions as compared to standard PINN.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a Physics-Informed Functional Link Constrained Framework with Domain Mapping (DFL-TFC) to analyze bending behavior of perforated nanobeams under sinusoidal loading, relating static bending to dynamic deflection efficiently without deep neural networks.
AI.motivation: To determine the relationship between static bending response and dynamic deflection of perforated nanobeams for various perforation cases, while avoiding complex deep network architectures.
AI.method: The FL-TFC with Domain Mapping method embeds governing differential equation constraints into a constrained expression (CE) using functional connections theory, maps the domain to orthogonal polynomials, and uses a functional link neural network (FLNN) to represent the free function. Static bending is solved via FL-TFC; dynamic deflection via Galerkin method.
AI.result: The DFL-TFC method eliminates the need for deep neural networks while ensuring accuracy, efficiency, and strict satisfaction of boundary conditions compared to standard PINNs. Relationship between static and dynamic deflection for simply-supported perforated nanobeams is established.
AI.conclusion: The proposed DFL-TFC method is an accurate and efficient alternative to standard PINNs for analyzing perforated nanobeam bending behavior, strictly satisfying boundary conditions without complex architectures.

title: Digital Twins in Coronary Artery Disease: A Mathematical Roadmap
summary: The combination of data and models, enhanced by AI methodologies, leads to the paradigm called Digital Twins. This concept is expected to bring unprecedented support to personalized medicine. The combination of mathematical and numerical models with diagnostic devices that provide patient-specific knowledge in a bidirectional framework can be a formidable decision support for clinicians. In this paper, we consider some mathematical aspects of constructing a Digital Twin to prevent and treat Coronary Artery Disease. The keywords for the bidirectional communication between twins in our system are (i) Data Assimilation and (ii) Probabilistic Graphic Models. In particular, a quantity of paramount interest in the evaluation and prognosis of Coronary Artery Disease is the Wall Shear Stress, i.e., the tangential component of normal stress on the arterial wall. By considering steps for the personalization and the synthesis of Wall Shear Stress estimation, we propose a mathematical roadmap for constructing a Digital Twin system that could help prevent infarcts, one of the most lethal diseases in the world.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a mathematical roadmap for constructing a Digital Twin system to prevent and treat Coronary Artery Disease, focusing on Wall Shear Stress estimation through Data Assimilation and Probabilistic Graphic Models.
AI.motivation: To leverage Digital Twins, enhanced by AI, as a decision-support tool for personalized medicine, specifically addressing the prevention and treatment of Coronary Artery Disease and infarcts, one of the world's most lethal diseases.
AI.method: The authors propose a mathematical framework combining (i) Data Assimilation and (ii) Probabilistic Graphic Models for bidirectional communication between physical and digital twins, with a focus on personalizing and synthesizing Wall Shear Stress estimation.
AI.result: A mathematical roadmap for constructing a Digital Twin system aimed at improving Wall Shear Stress estimation for Coronary Artery Disease evaluation and prognosis.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Digital Twin framework could serve as a formidable decision-support tool for clinicians, potentially helping prevent infarcts through better personalized assessment of cardiovascular risk.

title: A simple model of current ramp down in the ITER tokamak
summary: The controlled ramp down of the toroidal plasma current in the ITER tokamak is simulated using a simple model that employs cylindrical geometry. The magnetohydrodynamical (MHD) stability of the plasma throughout the whole current ramp is also calculated. The only potentially unstable MHD mode is the m=2/n=1 classical tearing mode. The envisioned 60 second ramp down of the plasma current in ITER is found to be perfectly feasible, provided that the plasma is sufficiently hot at the start of the ramp. However, attempts to ramp down the current on a significantly faster time scale are predicted to excite 2/1 tearing modes that are likely to lock to the vacuum vessel, and trigger a disruption.

AI.tldr: This paper simulates the controlled ramp down of plasma current in ITER and finds that a 60-second ramp down is feasible if the plasma is hot enough, but faster ramp downs could trigger disruptive tearing modes.
AI.motivation: To assess the feasibility and stability of controlled plasma current ramp-down in ITER, specifically investigating the risk of MHD instabilities such as tearing modes that could lead to disruptions.
AI.method: A simple cylindrical geometry model is used to simulate the toroidal plasma current ramp-down in ITER, along with MHD stability calculations focusing on classical tearing modes.
AI.result: The 60-second ramp-down is feasible if the plasma is sufficiently hot initially. Faster ramp-downs are predicted to excite m=2/n=1 tearing modes, which may lock to the vacuum vessel and cause disruptions.
AI.conclusion: Controlled current ramp-down in ITER is achievable within the planned 60-second timeframe under adequate thermal conditions, but accelerated ramp-down poses significant disruption risks due to unstable tearing modes.

title: Sharp Criteria for the existence of positive solutions to Lane-Emden-type inequalities on weighted graphs
summary:
We study positive solutions of Lane--Emden-type inequalities on infinite, connected, locally finite weighted graphs. For arbitrary connected domains (with Dirichlet boundary when present), we establish the equivalence between \[ -Δu \ge σu^q \] and the associated Green potential inequality. In particular, existence of positive solutions is characterized by the pointwise condition \[ G_Ω\big(σg_Ω(o,\cdot)^q\big)(x) \le C\, g_Ω(o,x). \] This yields a graph analogue of Green-kernel criteria for superlinear elliptic inequalities, without requiring a separate weak maximum principle.
  Our main result resolves a volume-growth conjecture: for any infinite, connected, locally finite weighted graph, if \[ \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{n^{2q-1}}{μ(B(o,n))^{q-1}} = \infty, \] then every nonnegative solution of \( -Δu \ge u^q \) is trivial. This nonexistence result requires no volume doubling, Poincaré inequality, or ellipticity assumptions. The proof combines finite-network Green-function methods, a unit-current decomposition, and a Hardy-type estimate.
  We also derive sharp existence criteria under \textnormal{(VD)}, \textnormal{(PI)}, and \textnormal{(P$_0$)}, and, under the \textnormal{(3G)} condition, obtain a criterion via Green level sets. As applications, we determine Serrin-type critical exponents on \(\mathbb{Z}^d\), including half-spaces and orthants with Dirichlet boundary conditions.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes equivalence between Lane-Emden inequalities and Green potential inequalities on weighted graphs, resolves a volume-growth conjecture for nonexistence of positive solutions, and derives sharp existence criteria under geometric conditions.
AI.motivation: To extend classical Green-kernel criteria for superlinear elliptic inequalities from Euclidean domains to infinite weighted graphs, and to characterize when positive solutions exist or are trivial without requiring strong geometric assumptions like volume doubling or Poincaré inequality.
AI.method: Combines finite-network Green-function methods, unit-current decomposition, Hardy-type estimates, and analysis under geometric conditions (VD, PI, P₀, 3G) to establish equivalence between the differential inequality and a Green potential inequality.
AI.result: (1) Equivalence between -Δu ≥ σu^q and a Green potential inequality on arbitrary domains. (2) Resolution of volume-growth conjecture: if ∑ n^{2q-1}/μ(B(o,n))^{q-1} = ∞, then all nonnegative solutions of -Δu ≥ u^q are trivial. (3) Sharp existence criteria under (VD), (PI), (P₀), and (3G) conditions. (4) Serrin-type critical exponents on ℤ^d, half-spaces, and orthants.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a complete graph-theoretic analogue of classical Euclidean results for Lane-Emden inequalities, with sharp criteria for existence/nonexistence and applications to lattice domains, advancing the theory of nonlinear elliptic equations on graphs.

title: Uncovering Exotic Paired States in the 2D Spin-Imbalanced Fermi Gas with Neural Wave Functions
summary: We study the zero-temperature phase diagram of the 2D spin-imbalanced Fermi gas with short-ranged attractive interactions using the recently developed neural network variational Monte Carlo method with the AGPs FermiNet Ansatz. The Fulde-Ferrell-Larkin-Ovchinnikov phase is observed in the weakly interacting BCS limit and a polarised superfluid is seen in the strongly interacting BEC limit. When the interactions are strong, the minority-spin momentum density is reduced almost to zero in the momentum-space region occupied by the unpaired majority-spin electrons. When the interactions are very strong, phase separation occurs, with regions containing bosonic pairs and unpaired regions occupied by the remaining majority-spin particles. In addition, we observe translational symmetry breaking at intermediate interaction strengths, where the system forms an exotic crystal of Cooper pairs in a Fermi fluid of unpaired majority-spin particles. We provide a possible explanation for the formation of the crystalline phase, explain the origins of the k-space momentum-density hole when the pairs are tightly bound, and discuss how our approach opens new directions for future work.

AI.tldr: This paper uses neural network variational Monte Carlo with the AGPs FermiNet Ansatz to map the zero-temperature phase diagram of 2D spin-imbalanced Fermi gases with attractive interactions, discovering the FFLO phase in the BCS limit, a polarized superfluid in the BEC limit, and an exotic Cooper pair crystal at intermediate interaction strengths, along with phase separation at very strong interactions.
AI.motivation: To understand the zero-temperature phase diagram of 2D spin-imbalanced Fermi gases with short-range attractive interactions, particularly the nature of exotic phases like the FFLO phase, using a novel computational approach (neural network variational Monte Carlo) that can capture complex quantum correlations.
AI.method: Neural network variational Monte Carlo method with the AGPs FermiNet Ansatz.
AI.result: (1) FFLO phase observed in the weakly interacting BCS limit. (2) Polarized superfluid seen in the strongly interacting BEC limit. (3) Minority-spin momentum density reduced to zero in momentum-space region occupied by unpaired majority-spin electrons under strong interactions. (4) Phase separation occurs at very strong interactions. (5) Translational symmetry breaking at intermediate interaction strengths, forming an exotic crystal of Cooper pairs in a Fermi fluid of unpaired majority-spin particles.
AI.conclusion: The neural network approach successfully resolves the phase diagram of 2D spin-imbalanced Fermi gases, revealing new exotic phases such as a Cooper pair crystal. The authors provide explanations for the crystalline phase formation and the momentum-space density hole, and suggest that their method opens new directions for future work on strongly correlated fermionic systems.

title: Cylindrical Projections of Occupied Diffusions
summary: Occupied diffusions offer a Markovian framework for path-dependent dynamics by lifting the state space with a flow of occupation measures. Because this additional feature is infinite-dimensional, the simulation of these processes remains computationally intractable. We address this by introducing \textit{cylindrical projections}, which approximate the occupation flow via a finite-dimensional system. We establish the strong convergence of this approximation to the initial process and derive corresponding convergence rates. The method is validated through Euler--Maruyama simulations of self-interacting diffusions and an application to the Local Occupied Volatility (LOV) model in finance. Finally, we provide a weak error analysis and explore its consequences for Monte Carlo methods and derivatives pricing.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces cylindrical projections to approximate infinite-dimensional occupied diffusions via finite-dimensional systems, proving strong convergence with rates, and validates through simulations and financial applications.
AI.motivation: Occupied diffusions are path-dependent but computationally intractable due to infinite-dimensional occupation measures; the paper aims to make them simulable.
AI.method: Cylindrical projections are used to approximate the occupation flow with a finite-dimensional system; strong convergence and rates are established analytically.
AI.result: Strong convergence of the approximation is proven with derived convergence rates; validated via Euler-Maruyama simulations of self-interacting diffusions and the LOV model.
AI.conclusion: The method enables tractable simulation of occupied diffusions, with weak error analysis supporting Monte Carlo methods and derivatives pricing.

title: Knudsen number as a non-thermal parameter: possible origin of skewness in space plasma distributions
comment: 23 pages, 3 figures, accepted for publication in The Astrophysical Journal
summary: Non-Maxwellian distributions and their origins in space plasma have attracted significant attention due to their prevalence and impact on various astrophysical and space-related phenomena. This paper presents a theoretical study of the consequences of incorporating a Skew-Kappa distribution to describe the non-thermal electron distribution in the solar wind. By introducing a Krook-like term into the Boltzmann equation to represent collision effects, we investigate the dependence of the skewness parameter on plasma macro-dynamics. Our analysis focuses on understanding the departure from thermal equilibrium and the statistical behavior of the plasma under the influence of collisional processes. By analyzing the Boltzmann transport equation adapted to space plasma, we derive expressions for the skewness parameter as a function of plasma parameters and the collision effect. Our results provide valuable information on the relationship between skewness, collisional dynamics, and the statistical properties of space plasmas, namely $δ\sim K_N$, the relationship between the skewness parameter and the effective Knudsen number. This study contributes to a deeper understanding of non-Maxwellian distributions and their role in astrophysical and space plasma phenomena.

AI.tldr: This paper theoretically studies how a Skew-Kappa distribution describes non-thermal electrons in the solar wind, deriving that the skewness parameter δ is proportional to the effective Knudsen number (K_N) using a Boltzmann equation with a Krook collision term.
AI.motivation: Non-Maxwellian distributions are prevalent in space plasma and significantly impact astrophysical phenomena, yet their origins and consequences are not fully understood, motivating a theoretical study of the Skew-Kappa distribution in the solar wind.
AI.method: The authors incorporate a Krook-like collision term into the Boltzmann equation to model collision effects, then analyze the Boltzmann transport equation adapted to space plasma, deriving expressions for the skewness parameter as a function of plasma parameters and collision effects.
AI.result: The skewness parameter δ is proportional to the effective Knudsen number (δ ∼ K_N), establishing a clear relationship between skewness, collisional dynamics, and the statistical properties of space plasmas.
AI.conclusion: This study advances the understanding of non-Maxwellian distributions and their role in astrophysical and space plasma phenomena, particularly by linking the skewness parameter to collisional transport properties.

title: Pollicott-Ruelle Resonances on Flag Manifolds
summary: We study the resonance spectrum of the multiflow induced on a flag manifold by the action, through multiplication by the exponential map, of the Cartan subalgebra of the underlying Lie group. We give a definition of joint resonance for the flow, then prove its discreteness and existence of resonant states. We conclude by explicit characterization of the spectrum in the special cases of Projective spaces and manifolds of full flags.

AI.tldr: This paper studies the resonance spectrum of multiflows on flag manifolds induced by Cartan subalgebra actions, proving discreteness and existence of resonant states with explicit characterization for projective spaces and full flag manifolds.
AI.motivation: To understand the resonance spectrum of multiflows on flag manifolds, specifically those induced by the action of the Cartan subalgebra via exponential multiplication, and to establish foundational properties like discreteness and existence of resonant states.
AI.method: The paper defines joint resonance for the multiflow, then proves discreteness and existence of resonant states through mathematical analysis. Special cases (projective spaces and full flag manifolds) are characterized explicitly.
AI.result: Joint resonance is defined, its discreteness is proved, and the existence of resonant states is established. Explicit spectral characterization is provided for projective spaces and full flag manifolds.
AI.conclusion: The resonance spectrum for multiflows on flag manifolds is discrete with existing resonant states, and can be explicitly characterized in special cases like projective spaces and full flag manifolds.

title: Quantum Dynamics via Score Matching on Bohmian Trajectories
summary: We solve the time-dependent Schrödinger equation by learning the score function, the gradient of the log-probability density, on Bohmian trajectories. In Bohm's formulation of quantum mechanics, particles follow deterministic paths under the classical potential supplemented by a quantum potential depending on the score function of the evolving density. These non-crossing Bohmian trajectories form a continuous normalizing flow governed by the score. We parametrize the score with a neural network and minimize a self-consistent Fisher divergence between the network and the score of the resulting density. We prove that the zero-loss minimizer of this self-consistent objective recovers Schrödinger dynamics for nodeless wave functions, a condition naturally met in quantum vibrations of atoms. We demonstrate the approach on wavepacket splitting in a double-well potential and anharmonic vibrations of a Morse chain. By recasting real-time quantum dynamics as a self-consistent score-driven normalizing flow, this framework opens the time-dependent Schrödinger equation to the rapidly advancing toolkit of modern generative modeling.

AI.tldr: The paper solves the time-dependent Schrödinger equation by learning the score function (gradient of log-probability density) on Bohmian trajectories using a neural network, reformulating quantum dynamics as a self-consistent score-driven normalizing flow.
AI.motivation: To bridge quantum dynamics and modern generative modeling by finding an alternative formulation of the time-dependent Schrödinger equation that can leverage score-based generative models.
AI.method: The authors parametrize the score function with a neural network, minimize a self-consistent Fisher divergence between the network and the score of the evolving density, and use Bohmian trajectories as a continuous normalizing flow governed by the score.
AI.result: Proven that zero-loss minimizer of the self-consistent objective recovers Schrödinger dynamics for nodeless wave functions. Demonstrated on wavepacket splitting in a double-well potential and anharmonic vibrations of a Morse chain.
AI.conclusion: By recasting real-time quantum dynamics as a self-consistent score-driven normalizing flow, this framework opens the time-dependent Schrödinger equation to modern generative modeling techniques.

title: A nonlocal coupled system: analysis and discretization
summary: We analyze a nonlocal coupled system arising as the Euler--Lagrange equations of an energy functional involving regional fractional Laplacians of orders $s_1$ and $s_2$ ($ 0 < s_1,s_2 < 1$), each acting on a separate disjoint domain and coupled through a nonlocal interaction term depending on a kernel $J$. Under suitable assumptions on the domains and the kernel, we prove existence and uniqueness of the energy minimizer and derive regularity estimates in fractional Sobolev spaces. We introduce a finite element discretization and establish a priori error estimates. We develop an alternating Schwarz-type method for both the continuous and discrete problems and prove its geometric convergence. Numerical experiments validate the theoretical predictions and illustrate the performance of the method.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes a nonlocal coupled system with regional fractional Laplacians on disjoint domains coupled via a kernel, proving existence/uniqueness of minimizers, regularity estimates, finite element error bounds, and geometric convergence of an alternating Schwarz method, validated by numerical experiments.
AI.motivation: To analyze a nonlocal coupled system arising from Euler--Lagrange equations of an energy functional with regional fractional Laplacians on disjoint domains and a nonlocal interaction term, establishing theoretical foundations and numerical methods.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis using fractional Sobolev spaces for existence, uniqueness, and regularity; finite element discretization with a priori error estimates; an alternating Schwarz-type method for continuous and discrete problems with geometric convergence proof; numerical validation.
AI.result: Existence and uniqueness of the energy minimizer, regularity estimates in fractional Sobolev spaces, a priori finite element error estimates, and geometric convergence of the alternating Schwarz method.
AI.conclusion: The proposed theoretical and numerical framework effectively handles the nonlocal coupled system, with numerical experiments confirming the theoretical predictions and demonstrating method performance.

title: Quasilinear flux model consistent with gyrokinetic ordering
summary:
We propose a quasilinear (QL) flux model in which the saturation amplitude is uniquely determined
  using multiscale gyrokinetic ordering relations.
  The model is fully self-contained within a linear framework and does not rely on calibration
  against nonlinear simulations or mixing-length estimates.
  The wavenumber-dependent flux is given in ion gyro-Bohm units with a weighting factor of $|k_θρ_i|$,
  such that its area integral in the log-linear scale yields the total flux, as employed in multiscale simulations.
  In systems with comparable ion and electron temperature gradients,
  the QL ion energy flux reproduces nonlinear simulation results in both its wavenumber dependence and absolute magnitude.
  In contrast, the QL electron flux is predominantly generated at electron scales,
  indicating that the shift of electron-scale transport toward ion scales observed in nonlinear Gsimulations
  is not captured within the present linear framework.
  We argue that the relation $Q_i\sim Q_e$,
  obtained as a closed conclusion of the QL model,
  may be predictive of simulation results if the area-integrated flux is conserved in nonlinear energy cascade process.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a quasilinear flux model that determines saturation amplitude using multiscale gyrokinetic ordering, without calibration against nonlinear simulations. The model reproduces ion energy flux well but fails to capture electron-scale transport shift observed in nonlinear simulations. It suggests $Q_i\sim Q_e$ may be predictive if area-integrated flux is conserved.
AI.motivation: To develop a self-contained quasilinear flux model that does not rely on calibration against nonlinear simulations or mixing-length estimates, enabling prediction of turbulent transport in fusion plasmas.
AI.method: Uses multiscale gyrokinetic ordering relations to uniquely determine saturation amplitude. The wavenumber-dependent flux is expressed in ion gyro-Bohm units with a $|k_θρ_i|$ weighting factor, and area-integrated in log-linear scale.
AI.result: QL ion energy flux reproduces nonlinear simulation results in wavenumber dependence and absolute magnitude for comparable ion/electron temperature gradients. QL electron flux is generated at electron scales, failing to capture the shift to ion scales seen in nonlinear simulations.
AI.conclusion: The relation $Q_i\sim Q_e$ from the QL model may be predictive if area-integrated flux is conserved in nonlinear energy cascade, though electron-scale transport shift is not captured.

title: Boundary estimates for the fractional spherical maximal function
summary: In this article, we study the fractional spherical maximal function and its lacunary counterpart. We study the necessary and sufficient conditions for $L^p-L^q$ boundedness of both maximal functions. In particular, we prove the restricted weak type estimate for both full and lacunary fractional spherical maximal functions at the boundary of the maximal $L^p-L^q$ bounded regions.

AI.tldr: The paper studies fractional spherical maximal functions and their lacunary versions, establishing necessary and sufficient conditions for Lp-Lq boundedness and proving restricted weak type estimates at boundary regions.
AI.motivation: To determine the exact conditions under which fractional spherical maximal functions are bounded between Lp and Lq spaces, particularly at the boundary of the maximal bounded region where standard estimates fail.
AI.method: The authors analyze necessary and sufficient conditions for Lp-Lq boundedness of both full and lacunary fractional spherical maximal functions, and prove restricted weak type estimates at the boundary.
AI.result: Restricted weak type estimates are proven for both full and lacunary fractional spherical maximal functions at the boundary of the maximal Lp-Lq bounded regions, alongside the complete characterization of boundedness.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a complete picture of Lp-Lq boundedness for fractional spherical maximal functions, including sharp results at the boundary cases.

title: Adaptive Sensing beyond Non-Adaptive Information Limits: End-to-End Co-Design of Geometry, Policy, and Inference
summary: Inverse design has made vast physical parameter spaces a substrate for emergent behavior. In sensing, the stakes of this principle are sharpest at the analog-to-digital boundary, where any information the hardware fails to capture is information no downstream algorithm can recover; hardware optimization alone is therefore not enough, and the geometry must be co-designed with a rule for what to measure next. We formulate this co-design as \emph{joint dynamic programming} (joint-DP): a single optimization over the continuous hardware geometry and a Bellman-optimal adaptive measurement policy. The outer hardware gradient is computed by differentiable dynamic programming with a sharp Bellman maximum, which the envelope theorem makes exact and bias-free, and a relaxation hierarchy carries the common framework from small discrete POMDPs to $10^5$-pixel photonic topologies. Across three case studies, joint-DP beats the natural baseline of its community by a large factor: on a radar beam-search POMDP, classical information-bound-guided geometry selection loses $2.8\times$ in attainable adaptive value; on a superconducting-qubit flux sensor, joint-DP reduces deployed mean-squared error by $11.3\times$ over the joint Bayesian Cramér--Rao baseline, with both geometries numerically co-optimized under their respective objectives; and on a $90{,}000$-pixel photonic metasensor, joint-DP via the Bayesian Fisher-information-matrix surrogate reduces deployed mean-squared error by $123\times$ relative to a randomized baseline. For any sensor whose hardware is designed once but whose policy runs for the device's lifetime, joint optimization of hardware and policy is the minimum principled procedure.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces joint dynamic programming (joint-DP), a framework that co-optimizes sensor hardware geometry and adaptive measurement policies simultaneously, demonstrating dramatic improvements over baselines in radar, superconducting qubit, and photonic metasensor applications.
AI.motivation: Hardware optimization alone is insufficient for sensing at the analog-to-digital boundary because any information the hardware fails to capture is permanently lost. The geometry must be co-designed with a policy for what to measure next.
AI.method: Joint-DP formulates a single optimization over continuous hardware geometry and a Bellman-optimal adaptive measurement policy. The hardware gradient uses differentiable dynamic programming with a sharp Bellman maximum, made exact via the envelope theorem. A relaxation hierarchy extends this from small discrete POMDPs to large-scale photonic topologies (10^5 pixels).
AI.result: Joint-DP beats baselines by large factors: 2.8× in radar beam-search POMDP, 11.3× MSE reduction in superconducting qubit flux sensing, and 123× MSE reduction in 90,000-pixel photonic metasensor compared to randomized baseline.
AI.conclusion: For any sensor whose hardware is designed once but whose policy runs for the device's lifetime, joint optimization of hardware and policy is the minimum principled procedure.

title: A Posteriori Error Estimation for Parabolic Equations with Enriched Galerkin Finite Element Methods
summary: This paper introduces a novel a posteriori error estimation framework for the enriched Galerkin (EG) finite element method applied to linear parabolic equations. While the EG method has been recognized for its local conservation property and computational efficiency compared to discontinuous Galerkin methods, its mathematical analysis in the context of a posteriori error estimation for parabolic problems remains unexplored. In this work, we prove reliability and efficiency using the residual-based approach. Furthermore, we integrate these error estimators into an adaptive mesh refinement strategy, demonstrating their effectiveness in achieving efficient and reliable error control through several numerical examples. The proposed approach provides a significant advancement in the mathematical foundation and practical applicability of the EG method for time-dependent problems.

AI.tldr: This paper presents the first a posteriori error estimation framework for the enriched Galerkin method applied to linear parabolic equations, proving reliability and efficiency via residual-based approach, and demonstrating effectiveness in adaptive mesh refinement.
AI.motivation: The EG method lacks mathematical analysis for a posteriori error estimation in parabolic problems despite its known advantages in local conservation and computational efficiency.
AI.method: Residual-based a posteriori error estimation framework with proven reliability and efficiency bounds, integrated into adaptive mesh refinement strategies.
AI.result: Numerical examples demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed error estimators in achieving efficient and reliable error control through adaptive mesh refinement.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach advances both the mathematical foundation and practical applicability of the EG method for time-dependent problems.

title: Wave-number-dependent closure condition for fluid moment equations
summary: Fluid models offer crucial computational efficiency for plasma simulations, yet accurately capturing kinetic effects like Landau damping remains a fundamental challenge. While conventional closures (e.g., Hammett-Perkins and Hunana) are widely used, their fidelity relative to exact kinetic simulations degrades significantly depending on the perturbation wave number. Here, we propose a novel wave-number-dependent closure condition for the three-moment fluid equations that explicitly preserves the primary dispersion relation. By mapping Padé approximant coefficients directly to the kinetic roots of the collisionless Vlasov-Poisson system, we derive an analytical closure that dynamically adapts to specific wave numbers. We further demonstrate that this framework readily extends to collisional plasmas via the BGK model. Numerical benchmarks confirm that this approach substantially improves the long-term accuracy of electric field energy and fluid moment evolution, offering a potential paradigm shift for high-fidelity fluid modeling.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a wave-number-dependent closure condition for three-moment fluid equations that preserves the primary dispersion relation by mapping Padé approximant coefficients to kinetic roots, improving accuracy of fluid plasma simulations for both collisionless and collisional (BGK) regimes.
AI.motivation: Fluid models are computationally efficient for plasma simulations but fail to capture kinetic effects like Landau damping accurately, especially as perturbation wave numbers vary. Conventional closures degrade in fidelity compared to exact kinetic simulations.
AI.method: The authors derive a wave-number-dependent closure condition for three-moment fluid equations by mapping Padé approximant coefficients directly to the kinetic roots of the collisionless Vlasov-Poisson system, and extend the framework to collisional plasmas via the BGK model.
AI.result: Numerical benchmarks show substantially improved long-term accuracy of electric field energy and fluid moment evolution compared to conventional closures.
AI.conclusion: The proposed wave-number-dependent closure offers a potential paradigm shift for high-fidelity fluid modeling of plasmas, bridging the gap between computational efficiency and kinetic accuracy.

title: The transmission problem with imperfect interfaces of small resistance
summary: We consider the transmission problem in presence of interfaces with imperfect bonding. The imperfect bonding condition is characterized by the positive resistance along the interface, which causes discontinuity of the potential across the interface while the flux is continuous. If the interface resistance is zero, then the interface is of perfect bonding, where both the potential and the flux of the solution are continuous across the interface. In this paper, we first construct using layer potentials the solution to the transmission problem with imperfect interfaces. We then prove that the solutions converge in various Sobolev spaces to the solution to the transmission problem with perfect interfaces as the interface resistance tends to zero. In particular, it is shown that the gradient of the solution converges in the uniform norm if the boundary is sufficiently regular.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes transmission problems with imperfect bonding interfaces, where resistance causes potential discontinuity but continuous flux. Using layer potentials, the authors construct solutions and prove convergence to perfect bonding solutions as resistance approaches zero, with gradient convergence in uniform norm for sufficiently regular boundaries.
AI.motivation: To understand how solutions to transmission problems with imperfect (resistive) interfaces behave as the interface resistance vanishes, and to mathematically prove convergence to the perfect bonding case.
AI.method: The authors use layer potentials to construct solutions to the transmission problem with imperfect interfaces, then analyze convergence properties as interface resistance tends to zero.
AI.result: The solutions converge in various Sobolev spaces to the perfect-interface solution. The gradient converges in the uniform norm when the boundary is sufficiently regular.
AI.conclusion: Transmission problems with imperfect bonding (resistive interfaces) converge to perfect bonding problems as resistance approaches zero, with strong gradient convergence under regularity conditions.

title: Kohn-Sham Hamiltonian from Effective Field Theory: Quasiparticle Band Narrowing from Frozen Core Dynamics
summary: Kohn-Sham (KS) eigenvalues are routinely compared with angle-resolved photoemission (ARPES) and used as input for many-body methods, yet density functional theory (DFT) assigns them no physical meaning. For alkali and alkaline-earth metals, KS bandwidths overestimate ARPES measurements by 20-35%, a discrepancy that persists across all exchange-correlation functionals. We construct an effective field theory (EFT) of the inhomogeneous electron gas and show that two conditions imply KS bands are the quasiparticle bands, up to a frozen-core renormalization factor zcore: a scale separation between core excitation energies and the valence Fermi energy, and an approximate Galilean invariance of the uniform electron gas confirmed by diagrammatic Monte Carlo. This factor reflects dynamical core excitations that conventional pseudopotentials freeze out and no static potential can capture. The correction 1-zcore reaches 20-35% for alkali metals but falls below 5% for Al and Si, explaining both the failure and success of KS band theory. We derive a closed-form post-SCF formula and validate it for Li, Na, K, Ca, Mg, Al, and Si; the predicted quasiparticle bands resolve the long-standing ARPES bandwidth discrepancy, matching embedded dynamical mean-field theory at negligible cost. This work also exemplifies first-principles agentic science, a direction particularly suited to the AGI-for-Science paradigm: an LLM-co-developed derivation with controlled approximations, verified symbolically and against a few experiments, becomes a deterministic harness for agentic scale-out, resolving simultaneously the LLM audit bottleneck and the non-falsifiability of fit-based AI-for-science.

AI.tldr: This paper resolves the long-standing discrepancy (20-35%) between Kohn-Sham band widths and ARPES measurements in alkali metals by constructing an effective field theory of the inhomogeneous electron gas. The authors show KS bands are quasiparticle bands up to a frozen-core renormalization factor zcore, deriving a closed-form post-SCF correction that matches embedded DMFT at negligible cost. The work is also presented as an example of LLM-co-developed first-principles agentic science.
AI.motivation: Kohn-Sham eigenvalues lack formal physical meaning yet are routinely compared to ARPES measurements, leading to systematic bandwidth overestimates of 20-35% for alkali and alkaline-earth metals that persist across all exchange-correlation functionals. The authors aim to resolve this fundamental discrepancy and provide theoretical justification for KS-ARPES comparison.
AI.method: The authors construct an effective field theory (EFT) of the inhomogeneous electron gas. They show that scale separation between core excitation energies and valence Fermi energy, plus approximate Galilean invariance of the uniform electron gas (confirmed by diagrammatic Monte Carlo), imply KS bands are quasiparticle bands up to a frozen-core renormalization factor zcore. They derive a closed-form post-SCF formula accounting for dynamical core excitations that conventional pseudopotentials freeze out.
AI.result: The correction 1-zcore reaches 20-35% for alkali metals but falls below 5% for Al and Si, explaining the failure and success of KS band theory. The formula is validated for Li, Na, K, Ca, Mg, Al, and Si, resolving the ARPES bandwidth discrepancy and matching embedded DMFT at negligible computational cost.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a clean, physically grounded resolution to a decades-old puzzle in condensed matter physics, and also exemplifies a new paradigm of LLM-co-developed agentic science where controlled approximations, symbolic verification, and minimal experimental validation replace the audit bottleneck and non-falsifiability of fit-based AI-for-science.

title: Numerical Analysis of Stochastic Elliptic Variational Inequalities of the First Kind
summary: This paper presents a numerical approach to the stochastic obstacle problem using the stochastic Galerkin (SG) method. Due to the low regularity of the solution, linear finite elements are employed in both the physical and random variable spaces. Properties of random fields and variational inequalities of the first kind are employed to establish the well-posedness of the problem. Finite element spaces are introduced to construct suitable approximation subspaces, and a comprehensive SG formulation is proposed to solve the stochastic obstacle problem. Well-posedness of the discrete formulation is shown and an optimal error estimate for the numerical solution in the $H^1$-norm is derived. Numerical experiments validate the effectiveness of the SG method, showing that both the expectation error and second moment error converge at a rate of $O(h)$ in the $H^1$-norm, consistent with theoretical predictions.

AI.tldr: This paper applies the stochastic Galerkin method to solve the stochastic obstacle problem, using linear finite elements for spatial and random spaces, proving well-posedness and optimal H¹-norm error estimates, with numerical validation showing O(h) convergence.
AI.motivation: To develop and analyze a numerical method for the stochastic obstacle problem, which involves low solution regularity, by employing the stochastic Galerkin method with proper theoretical guarantees.
AI.method: Uses stochastic Galerkin (SG) method with linear finite elements in both physical and stochastic dimensions. Employs random field properties and variational inequalities to ensure well-posedness, constructs suitable finite element approximation subspaces, and develops a comprehensive SG formulation.
AI.result: Optimal error estimate in H¹-norm is derived and numerically validated. Both expectation error and second moment error converge at O(h) rate in H¹-norm, matching theoretical predictions.
AI.conclusion: The SG method is effective for the stochastic obstacle problem, with proven well-posedness and optimal convergence rates confirmed by numerical experiments.

title: Second Harmonic Generation Through Backward Raman Scattering in Magnetized Plasmas Driven by Circularly Polarized Intense Lasers
summary: A fluid-based theoretical framework is developed to describe the nonlinear cascade linking primary BRS-driven plasma wave amplification, oscillating two-stream instability (OTSI), nonlinear current generation within a self-formed ponderomotive channel, and radiation of the secondary electromagnetic mode. Systematic parameter studies reveal a strong sensitivity of the entire cascade to the relative handedness of laser polarization and axial magnetic field direction, as well as to cyclotron resonance strength. Resonant right-handed circular polarization significantly enhances ponderomotive expulsion, channel depth, BRS and OTSI growth rates, nonlinear current density, and the amplitude of the secondary harmonic, whereas non-resonant left-handed polarization effectively suppresses these processes. Fully kinetic particle in cell simulations using EPOCH, together with macroscopic finite-element modelling in COMSOL Multiphysics, corroborate the polarization- and magnetization dependent wake modulation and channelling efficiency across a wide range of laser wavelengths, pulse durations, and plasma densities. The temporal evolution and saturation of OTSI, resilience to density inhomogeneities, and wavenumber-resolved resonance tuning illustrate axial magnetization as a flexible control mechanism for adjusting the intensity, spectral position, bandwidth, and stability of multi peak Raman spectra. These findings demonstrate that cyclotron resonance and polarization control are effective methods for manipulating nonlinear Raman emission in high-intensity laser magnetized plasma interactions, offering predictive insights for forthcoming kinetic simulations and experimental implementations.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a fluid-based theoretical framework to describe the nonlinear cascade from BRS-driven plasma wave amplification through OTSI, nonlinear current generation, and secondary electromagnetic radiation in magnetized plasmas. It shows that resonant right-handed circular polarization enhances the cascade while left-handed polarization suppresses it, validated by PIC simulations (EPOCH) and finite-element modelling (COMSOL), demonstrating polarization and cyclotron resonance as effective control mechanisms for nonlinear Raman emission.
AI.motivation: To understand and control the nonlinear cascade processes (BRS, OTSI, nonlinear currents, secondary radiation) in high-intensity laser-magnetized plasma interactions, and to identify how polarization handedness and cyclotron resonance can manipulate these processes for predictable Raman emission control.
AI.method: A fluid-based theoretical framework describing the cascade from BRS-driven plasma wave amplification → OTSI → nonlinear current generation → secondary EM radiation. Systematic parameter studies of polarization handedness and cyclotron resonance. Validated by fully kinetic PIC simulations (EPOCH) and macroscopic finite-element modelling (COMSOL Multiphysics) across varying laser wavelengths, pulse durations, and plasma densities.
AI.result: Resonant right-handed circular polarization significantly enhances ponderomotive expulsion, channel depth, BRS and OTSI growth rates, nonlinear current density, and secondary harmonic amplitude. Left-handed polarization suppresses these processes. Axial magnetization serves as a flexible control mechanism for intensity, spectral position, bandwidth, and stability of multi-peak Raman spectra.
AI.conclusion: Cyclotron resonance and polarization control are effective methods for manipulating nonlinear Raman emission in high-intensity laser magnetized plasma interactions, providing predictive insights for future kinetic simulations and experimental implementations.

title: Uniqueness of simultaneous reconstruction of general space- and time-dependent sources and initial states in fractional diffusion equations and systems from single boundary measurements
summary: Inverse problem to determine simultaneously a general space- and time-dependent source and an initial state in a fractional diffusion equation from an {\it a posteriori} measurement of the normal derivative of the state on a portion of a boundary of the space domain is considered. Uniqueness for this problem is proved under the assumption that an order of a fractional derivative involved in the equation is irrational. The uniqueness result is generalized to an inverse problem for a coupled system of fractional diffusion equations, where both sources and initial states are unknown and first component of the state is measured on the boundary.

AI.tldr: This paper studies an inverse problem in fractional diffusion equations, determining unknown space- and time-dependent sources and initial states from boundary measurements. Uniqueness is proved when the fractional derivative order is irrational, and the result extends to coupled systems.
AI.motivation: To address the inverse problem of simultaneously identifying a general source term and initial state in fractional diffusion equations using only boundary measurements—a challenging and practical issue in anomalous diffusion modeling.
AI.method: The authors prove uniqueness using analytical arguments under the assumption that the fractional derivative order is irrational, and generalize the approach to coupled systems of fractional diffusion equations.
AI.result: Uniqueness for the inverse problem is established when the fractional order is irrational, and the result extends to coupled systems where only the first component is measured on the boundary.
AI.conclusion: The irrationality of the fractional derivative order enables unique determination of both source and initial state, offering theoretical foundations for inverse problems in fractional diffusion models.

title: Hardware Realization of a Hamiltonian Simulation Algorithm for Time-Domain Maxwells Equations
summary: We present the first quantum-hardware implementation of a Hamiltonian simulation algorithm that produces signed vector-field solutions to the time-domain Maxwells equations using a Schrodingerisation-based approach.The electromagnetic fields are discretized using finite-difference operators, and the resulting non-unitary matrices are mapped to Bell-basis Trotter blocks, enabling efficient circuit construction. We introduce a measurement procedure that retrieves not only field amplitudes, but also physical directions of the electric and magnetic field values at select spatial points. Implementing this logic on quantum hardware relies on relative-phase-based sign reconstruction. Numerical results obtained using IonQ QPU, show good agreement with analytical solutions of benchmark problems in two dimensions and on simulators; in three dimensions. We further extend our approach to compute fields scattered from simple bodies, by enforcing appropriate boundary conditions. Our work lays the foundational steps towards realizing quantum-hardware solutions for computational electromagnetics.

AI.tldr: A quantum-hardware implementation of Hamiltonian simulation for solving Maxwell's equations using Schrodingerisation, demonstrated on IonQ QPU with good agreement to analytical solutions.
AI.motivation: To realize quantum-hardware solutions for computational electromagnetics by implementing a Hamiltonian simulation algorithm that solves Maxwell's equations with signed vector-field solutions.
AI.method: Uses Schrodingerisation-based approach with finite-difference discretization, maps non-unitary matrices to Bell-basis Trotter blocks, and employs relative-phase-based sign reconstruction for measurement.
AI.result: Numerical results from IonQ QPU show good agreement with analytical solutions in 2D, simulator validation in 3D, and extension to scattering problems with boundary conditions.
AI.conclusion: This work lays foundational steps toward quantum-hardware solutions for computational electromagnetics.

title: Encoded Forward Backward Stochastic Neural Network for High-Dimensional Backward Stochastic Differential Equations and Parabolic Partial Differential Equations
summary: Backward stochastic differential equation (BSDE) provides probabilistic solutions for a class of parabolic partial differential equations (PDEs). DeepBSDE and FBSNN are two deep learning approaches for solving high-dimensional PDEs through approximating the solution of BSDEs. The conventional approach for learning functions defined on continuous domains is via fully-connected networks (FCNs) such that each input dimension is represented by a single neuron. In the current study, a new encoded FBSNN algorithm is proposed to enhance the efficiency and accuracy of approximating BSDEs using encoding and convolution. The input coordinates are encoded as tensors treated as images with multiple channels which can be processed efficiently by convolutional neural networks. The encoding mechanism enriches the input features such that the spatial and temporal features can be balanced. The encoded FBSNN algorithm provides a simple yet effective extension of the vanilla FBSNN algorithm such that BSDEs can be approximated more efficiently. The new algorithm is validated using the essentially high-dimensional Black-Scholes-Barenblatt and Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman benchmark cases.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an encoded FBSNN algorithm that uses encoding and convolutional neural networks (instead of fully-connected networks) to improve the efficiency and accuracy of solving high-dimensional BSDEs/PDEs.
AI.motivation: Existing deep learning approaches (DeepBSDE, FBSNN) use fully-connected networks where each input dimension is a single neuron, which becomes inefficient for high-dimensional PDEs. The authors aim to enhance approximation efficiency and accuracy.
AI.method: The proposed encoded FBSNN algorithm encodes input coordinates as tensors (treated as multi-channel images) and processes them with convolutional neural networks instead of fully-connected networks, enriching input features and balancing spatial-temporal information.
AI.result: Validated on high-dimensional Black-Scholes-Barenblatt and Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman benchmark problems, showing the encoded FBSNN approximates BSDEs more efficiently than the vanilla FBSNN.
AI.conclusion: The encoded FBSNN is a simple yet effective extension of the vanilla FBSNN, improving approximation efficiency for high-dimensional BSDEs through encoding and convolution.

title: Theoretical Analysis and PIC Simulations of Electromagnetic Wakefields Excited by Relativistic Beams in Magnetized Plasmas
summary: This study presents theoretical and numerical investigation of the coupled longitudinal and radial wakefields excited by ultrarelativistic electron beams propagating through a cold plasma channel subjected to an external axial magnetic field. A fully causal three dimensional Green function formalism is developed directly from the linearized Maxwell fluid equations in the presence of the magnetized plasma dielectric tensor. This unified framework captures the complete electromagnetic response, including the induction of a transverse plasma current and the resulting hybridization of longitudinal charge separation dynamics with cyclotron driven transverse motion. The analytical treatment reveals how magnetization modifies the effective restoring forces, enhances wake amplitudes, and reshapes the radial focusing defocusing structure of the wake. To validate the theoretical predictions and explore realistic parameter regimes, extensive three dimensional particle in cell simulations are performed using the EPOCH code across wide ranges of plasma density, magnetic field strength, beam Lorentz factor, transverse beam radius, and longitudinal current profiles. The simulations demonstrate excellent quantitative agreement with the analytical Green function solutions, confirming that increasing plasma density substantially amplifies the initial wake amplitude while accelerating the damping of higher order oscillations. Application of an external magnetic field induces coherent high frequency radial oscillations, strengthens focusing forces, and produces a hybrid eigenmode whose properties are absent in the unmagnetized limit. Variations in the driver Lorentz factor lead to rapid convergence toward a universal ultrarelativistic wake structure, while the transverse beam profile controls the radial extent and balance between longitudinal acceleration and transverse focusing.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a 3D Green function formalism for magnetized plasma wakefields, showing that external magnetic fields enhance wake amplitudes, induce hybrid cyclotron-longitudinal modes, and reshape focusing structures—validated by PIC simulations across diverse parameter regimes.
AI.motivation: To understand and quantify how an external axial magnetic field modifies the coupled longitudinal and transverse wakefields excited by ultrarelativistic electron beams in plasma channels, particularly focusing on magnetization effects that are absent in conventional unmagnetized plasma wakefield theory.
AI.method: Fully causal 3D Green function formalism derived from linearized Maxwell-fluid equations with a magnetized plasma dielectric tensor, combined with extensive 3D particle-in-cell (PIC) simulations using the EPOCH code across varied plasma densities, magnetic fields, beam Lorentz factors, radii, and current profiles.
AI.result: (1) Increasing plasma density amplifies initial wake amplitude and damps higher-order oscillations; (2) External magnetic field induces coherent high-frequency radial oscillations, strengthens focusing forces, and produces hybrid eigenmodes unseen in unmagnetized cases; (3) Beam Lorentz factor converges rapidly to a universal ultrarelativistic wake; (4) Transverse beam profile controls radial extent and balance between acceleration and focusing.
AI.conclusion: Magnetized plasma wakefields exhibit richer dynamics than their unmagnetized counterparts, with external magnetic fields serving as an effective control parameter for enhancing wake amplitude, modifying focusing structures, and generating hybrid modes—validated by excellent quantitative agreement between theory and PIC simulations.

title: Path-dependent Hamilton--Jacobi equations: Uniqueness results for viscosity solutions defined via families of compact sets
summary: We consider a path-dependent Hamilton--Jacobi equation with coinvariant derivatives over the space of continuous functions. We prove two uniqueness results for viscosity (generalized) solutions defined in terms of coinvariantly smooth test functionals and a dense family of compact subsets of the space of continuous functions. It is assumed that the Hamiltonian is continuous and satisfies a local Lipschitz condition in the functional variable with respect to the supremum norm. When the Lipschitz constant satisfies a sublinear growth condition in the gradient (impulse) variable, uniqueness is established in the class of continuous viscosity solutions. In the general case, without any such growth conditions, uniqueness is established in the class of continuous viscosity solutions that satisfy an additional local Lipschitz condition. The proofs are based on the standard method of doubling variables, but use a novel penalty functional for constructing coinvariantly smooth test functionals. The obtained results generalize previously known ones by relaxing the assumptions on the Hamiltonian and/or enlarging the class of functionals in which uniqueness is established.

AI.tldr: The paper proves uniqueness of viscosity solutions for path-dependent Hamilton-Jacobi equations with coinvariant derivatives under relaxed conditions, using a novel penalty functional in the doubling variables method.
AI.motivation: To establish uniqueness results for viscosity solutions of path-dependent Hamilton-Jacobi equations with coinvariant derivatives, relaxing previous assumptions on the Hamiltonian and expanding the solution class.
AI.method: The standard method of doubling variables is adapted with a novel penalty functional to construct coinvariantly smooth test functionals. Two cases are considered: (1) Hamiltonian with sublinear growth in the gradient variable yields uniqueness in continuous viscosity solutions; (2) Without growth conditions, uniqueness holds in a class satisfying an additional local Lipschitz condition.
AI.result: Two uniqueness results are proved: (i) under sublinear growth of the Lipschitz constant in the gradient variable; (ii) in the general case without growth conditions, for solutions with an extra local Lipschitz condition. These generalize prior results by relaxing Hamiltonian assumptions and enlarging the functional class.
AI.conclusion: The paper extends uniqueness theory for path-dependent HJ equations with coinvariant derivatives, demonstrating broader applicability through relaxed conditions and a novel test functional construction.

title: A Continuous-Time Ensemble Kalman-Bucy Smoother for Causal Inference and Model Discovery
comment: 33 pages, 11 figures. Corresponding author: Nan Chen (chennan@math.wisc.edu)
summary: Data assimilation (DA) integrates observational information with model predictions to improve state estimation in complex systems. While filtering provides the basis for online forecasts by using only past and present observations, it can exhibit delays and biases when the underlying dynamics evolve rapidly or undergo regime transitions. Smoothing, which additionally incorporates future observations, provides a natural pipeline for hindcasting and reanalysis that yields an uncertainty reduction beyond the filter. This paper introduces an ensemble Kalman-Bucy smoother (EnKBS) for continuous-time DA of nonlinear dynamical systems, where the smoother's conditional distributions are reconstructed using ensemble moments. The result is a derivative-free framework that does not require explicit computation of tangent-linear or adjoint models, which converges to the exact smoother solution at the infinite-ensemble limit for a wide class of complex systems. Incorporating standard regularization techniques for high-dimensional systems, such as covariance localization and inflation, the skill of the EnKBS is demonstrated in various important scientific problems. By integrating future observations, which reveal the underlying causal mechanisms for retrospective state updates, the EnKBS is used for Bayesian-based inference of causal relationships and their temporal influence range in a dyadic trigger-feedback model and the development of a causality-driven iterative learning algorithm that identifies the structure and recovers the hidden parameters of a nonlinear reduced-order model mimicking midlatitude atmospheric circulation. Notably, both tasks remain effective with an ensemble size of $O(10)$ under partial observations, suggesting that EnKBS can support the instantaneous discovery of high-dimensional complex systems over time.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces an ensemble Kalman-Bucy smoother (EnKBS) for continuous-time data assimilation of nonlinear dynamical systems. The method uses ensemble moments to reconstruct conditional distributions without requiring tangent-linear or adjoint models, converges to the exact smoother solution at the infinite-ensemble limit, and demonstrates effectiveness in causal inference and parameter recovery tasks with small ensemble sizes (O(10)) under partial observations.
AI.motivation: Standard filtering methods for data assimilation can exhibit delays and biases when dynamics evolve rapidly or undergo regime transitions. Smoothing, which incorporates future observations, offers uncertainty reduction beyond filtering but existing methods often require computationally expensive tangent-linear or adjoint models, limiting their applicability to complex nonlinear systems.
AI.method: The EnKBS is a derivative-free ensemble Kalman-Bucy smoother for continuous-time DA. It reconstructs smoother conditional distributions using ensemble moments, eliminates the need for tangent-linear or adjoint models, and incorporates standard regularization techniques (covariance localization, inflation) for high-dimensional systems. The framework is applied to causal inference in a dyadic trigger-feedback model and to a causality-driven iterative learning algorithm for identifying structure and recovering hidden parameters of a nonlinear reduced-order atmospheric model.
AI.result: The EnKBS converges to the exact smoother solution at the infinite-ensemble limit and demonstrates skill across various scientific problems. Both causal inference and parameter recovery tasks remain effective with an ensemble size of O(10) under partial observations, suggesting the method can support instantaneous discovery of high-dimensional complex systems over time.
AI.conclusion: The EnKBS provides a practical, derivative-free smoothing framework for continuous-time DA that leverages future observations for retrospective state updates. Its effectiveness with small ensembles under partial observations highlights its potential for real-time discovery and causal analysis in high-dimensional complex systems.

title: Augmented reality system for visualising magnetic field topology and charged-particle trajectories in magnetic fusion plasmas
summary: A cost-effective augmented reality (AR) system is presented for visualising three-dimensional magnetic field structures and charged-particle trajectories in magnetically confined fusion plasmas. The system presented in this study integrates an orbit-following simulation code with a marker-based AR framework using a web camera and the OpenCV library. By synchronizing the time step of the simulation with the frame rate of the camera, the trajectories are continuously updated and superimposed in real time onto the camera image. Through the interactive operation of manipulating the web camera, users can observe three-dimensional structures, such as magnetic islands, from various positions and viewing angles. Simultaneously, the visualisation results can be shared by multiple people through a display. Such a shared AR environment supports an intuitive understanding of three-dimensional spatial structures that involve a high cognitive load. It also enables collaborative reasoning based on common visual information in research on magnetic confinement fusion, where researchers and students have diverse backgrounds in physics, engineering, and related fields.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a cost-effective augmented reality system that visualizes 3D magnetic field structures and charged-particle trajectories in fusion plasmas in real time, using a web camera and marker-based AR, enabling shared interactive observation from multiple angles.
AI.motivation: To reduce the cognitive load and support intuitive understanding of complex 3D spatial structures (e.g., magnetic islands) in magnetic confinement fusion research, especially for researchers and students with diverse backgrounds.
AI.method: An orbit-following simulation code is integrated with a marker-based AR framework using a web camera and OpenCV. The simulation time step is synchronized with the camera frame rate to update and superimpose trajectories in real time onto the camera image.
AI.result: Users can observe 3D structures from various positions and angles by manipulating the web camera. The visualization is shared via a display for collaborative viewing.
AI.conclusion: The shared AR environment facilitates intuitive understanding and collaborative reasoning based on common visual information for interdisciplinary fusion research teams.

title: Mathematical Models of Traffic Flow at a Signalized Intersection
summary: This paper presents two one-dimensional mathematical models describing automobile traffic flow on straight road segments at a signalized intersection. When the traffic light is permissive, the flow density and velocity are obtained by solving an initial-boundary value problem for a first-order hyperbolic system. When the signal is prohibitive, the same quantities are governed by a mixed system comprising a second-order parabolic equation for the velocity and a first-order equation for the density.

AI.tldr: This paper presents two one-dimensional mathematical models for traffic flow at a signalized intersection, using hyperbolic and parabolic systems depending on traffic light state.
AI.motivation: To mathematically model automobile traffic flow on straight road segments at signalized intersections, capturing different dynamics under permissive and prohibitive traffic light conditions.
AI.method: Two one-dimensional mathematical models are developed: (1) a first-order hyperbolic system for permissive (green) light conditions, and (2) a mixed system with a second-order parabolic equation for velocity and a first-order equation for density under prohibitive (red) light conditions.
AI.result: The flow density and velocity can be obtained by solving initial-boundary value problems for these systems under the respective traffic signal states.
AI.conclusion: Different mathematical structures (hyperbolic vs. parabolic) are required to accurately model traffic flow dynamics depending on whether the signal is permissive or prohibitive.

title: Numerical approximation of a transient thermo-electromagnetic problem in axisymmetric geometries
summary: This paper analyzes a transient thermo-electromagnetic problem arising in the modeling of induction heating processes. Unlike previous studies that focused on steady-state scenarios, we consider a time-dependent thermal problem coupled with a nonlinear time-harmonic electromagnetic problem through temperature-dependent electrical conductivity and Joule effect. Exploiting cylindrical symmetry and assuming a purely azimuthal current density, we formulate the problem on a two-dimensional meridional section. We introduce a variational formulation in appropriately weighted Sobolev spaces and prove existence of a solution by a fixed-point argument. Under reasonable assumptions on the physical parameters, we also prove uniqueness. A finite element discretization combined with implicit time stepping is used to compute the numerical solution. To evaluate the accuracy of the approximation, a priori error estimates are derived and validated by numerical experiments. Finally, numerical simulations illustrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach in an industrially relevant configuration.

AI.tldr: This paper develops and analyzes a mathematical model for transient induction heating, proving existence and uniqueness of solutions, and validating numerical methods with error estimates and industrial simulations.
AI.motivation: To extend the analysis of induction heating from steady-state to time-dependent (transient) scenarios, addressing the coupling between thermal and electromagnetic problems via temperature-dependent electrical conductivity.
AI.method: The authors formulate the problem in 2D using cylindrical symmetry, introduce variational formulations in weighted Sobolev spaces, prove existence via fixed-point argument and uniqueness under reasonable assumptions, and employ finite element discretization with implicit time stepping.
AI.result: A priori error estimates are derived and validated numerically. Numerical simulations demonstrate the approach's effectiveness in an industrially relevant configuration.
