AI.conclusion: The proposed framework successfully solves the transient coupled thermo-electromagnetic problem with proven theoretical guarantees and practical numerical performance.

title: Model-free interpretation of X-ray Thomson scattering measurements
summary: X-ray Thomson scattering (XRTS) has emerged as a widely used diagnostics for extreme states of matter in a great variety of situations, and over a broad range of parameters. The standard approach for the interpretation of XRTS measurements is given by the forward modeling approach, where the electronic dynamic structure factor $S_{ee}(\mathbf{q},ω)$ is computed from a suitable theoretical model, convolved with the combined source-and-instrument function, and then matched with the experimental observation, treating a-priori unknown parameters such as the mass density, temperature and ionization state as free fit parameters. Very recently, it has been suggested that this inherent model dependence can be avoided by analyzing XRTS spectra in terms of the imaginary-time correlation function (ITCF) $F_{ee}(\mathbf{q},τ)$ [Dornheim \textit{et al.}, \textit{Nature Commun.}~\textbf{13}, 7911 (2022)], giving one model-free access to the temperature, normalization, Rayleigh weight, as well as a number of other properties. Here, we present a comprehensive review article on these developments, including accessible discussions of the method's theoretical background in terms of Feynman's imaginary-time path integral picture of statistical mechanics as well as its remaining limitations, in particular with respect to the source-and-instrument function of the experimental set-up. In addition, we discuss new chances for the further development of this framework by utilizing emerging capabilities for high-repetition XRTS experiments with meV resolution over spectral ranges of tens of eV at state-of-the-art x-ray free electron laser (XFEL) facilities such as the European XFEL in Germany.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive review of a model-free approach (based on imaginary-time correlation functions) for analyzing X-ray Thomson scattering spectra, which avoids the traditional forward modeling and its inherent model dependencies.
AI.motivation: To overcome the inherent model dependence of traditional forward modeling approaches used to interpret X-ray Thomson scattering (XRTS) measurements, where unknown parameters (density, temperature, ionization state) must be treated as free fit parameters.
AI.method: The authors propose analyzing XRTS spectra via the imaginary-time correlation function (ITCF) $F_{ee}(\mathbf{q},τ)$, rooted in Feynman's imaginary-time path integral picture of statistical mechanics. This provides model-free access to temperature, normalization, Rayleigh weight, and other properties.
AI.result: The approach eliminates the need for forward modeling and free parameter fitting; however, limitations remain regarding the source-and-instrument function of the experimental setup. New opportunities arise with high-repetition XFEL facilities (e.g., European XFEL) offering meV resolution over tens of eV ranges.
AI.conclusion: The ITCF-based analysis represents a paradigm shift for XRTS diagnostics, though further development is needed, particularly in handling experimental source-and-instrument functions. Emerging XFEL capabilities promise to advance this framework.

title: Conditonal Lipschitz stability for the Inverse Problem of the 2D Navier-Stokes System in a Bounded Domain
summary: This paper concerns an inverse problem for the initial boundary value problem of the two-dimensional Navier-Stokes system defined in a bounded simply connected domain with slip, vorticity boundary conditions, and a global vorticity invariant constraint. We establish conditional Lipschitz stability and a local recovery for this inverse problem, where the velocity field and space-independent boundary vorticity are locally recovered from the given initial velocity field and the global vorticity invariant. Our analysis is based on well-posedness estimates and energy methods for the vorticity transport equation.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes conditional Lipschitz stability and local recovery for an inverse problem of the 2D Navier-Stokes system with slip boundary conditions, recovering velocity field and boundary vorticity from initial velocity and global vorticity invariant using well-posedness estimates and energy methods.
AI.motivation: To solve an inverse problem for the 2D Navier-Stokes system under slip/vorticity boundary conditions, aiming to establish stability and recovery guarantees for the velocity field and boundary vorticity.
AI.method: The authors employ well-posedness estimates and energy methods for the vorticity transport equation to analyze the inverse problem.
AI.result: Conditional Lipschitz stability and local recovery are established for the inverse problem.
AI.conclusion: The velocity field and space-independent boundary vorticity can be locally recovered from the initial velocity field and global vorticity invariant with Lipschitz-type stability.

title: Transmitted and Storage-Dominated Resonance in Fractionally Damped Unidirectionally Coupled Duffing Oscillators
summary: This paper investigates resonance transmission in two unidirectionally coupled Duffing oscillators with fractional damping, where the driver is harmonically forced and the receiver is connected through a linear coupling spring. Particular attention is paid to how fractional damping in the receiver modifies amplitude amplification, energy redistribution, and the structure of the coupled response. The numerical results reveal a clear distinction between transmitted resonance, associated with a coupling-power balance consistent with direct energy transfer through the coupling spring, and storage-dominated resonance, in which the receiver still exhibits a pronounced oscillatory response while the time-averaged coupling power becomes negative under the adopted convention. In this latter regime, fractional memory promotes temporary energy accumulation within the receiver--coupling subsystem, followed by partial release through the coupling spring without any feedback on the driver dynamics. We further show that detuning the receiver natural frequency enhances the interaction between the lower-frequency transmitted response and the higher-frequency coupled response, leading to a superposed resonance regime with increased receiver amplitude, stronger localization, and sharper response. The roles of the fractional order, coupling strength, and receiver natural frequency are systematically analyzed through frequency-response curves and parametric maps. Overall, the results show how fractional memory can be used to tune resonance transmission, energy localization, and amplified response in coupled nonlinear oscillators.

AI.tldr: This paper studies resonance transmission in two coupled Duffing oscillators with fractional damping, identifying transmitted resonance, storage-dominated resonance (where fractional memory causes energy accumulation and release), and superposed resonance regimes, showing how fractional order, coupling strength, and detuning control energy localization and amplification.
AI.motivation: To understand how fractional damping modifies amplitude amplification, energy redistribution, and response structure in coupled nonlinear oscillators, particularly in regimes where conventional resonance transmission behavior breaks down.
AI.method: Numerical simulation of two unidirectionally coupled Duffing oscillators with fractional damping, analyzing frequency-response curves and parametric maps across varying fractional orders, coupling strengths, and receiver natural frequencies.
AI.result: Identified three distinct regimes: (1) transmitted resonance with positive coupling power, (2) storage-dominated resonance with negative time-averaged coupling power due to fractional memory, and (3) superposed resonance when detuning enhances interaction between lower and higher frequency responses, increasing amplitude and localization.
AI.conclusion: Fractional memory can be leveraged to tune resonance transmission, energy localization, and amplified response in coupled nonlinear oscillators, offering control over distinct dynamical regimes.

title: Convergence analysis of a full discretization of operator-valued differential Riccati equations
summary:
In recent previous work [E. Hansen, T. Stillfjord and T. Åberg, SIAM J. Numer. Anal., to appear], we analyzed the convergence of operator splitting methods applied to operator-valued differential Riccati equations (DRE). In this paper, we extend these results by analyzing the convergence of a full discretization based on finite elements in space and Lie splitting in time. As far as we are aware, this is the first such analysis for DRE. There are very few analyses of temporal discretizations of DRE overall, and none of them have been combined with spatial discretizations. However, it is clearly vital to know when the full discretization converges, since this is what will be used in practical applications.
  Our main result is that except for logarithmic factors, the method converges with order one in time and order two in space, under fairly weak assumptions on the problem data. This is illustrated by a numerical experiment based on an application in optimal control.

AI.tldr: This paper extends previous convergence analysis of operator splitting methods for differential Riccati equations (DRE) to a full discretization using finite elements in space and Lie splitting in time, showing first-order convergence in time and second-order in space (except logarithmic factors) under weak assumptions.
AI.motivation: While previous work analyzed operator splitting for DRE, no existing analysis combined temporal and spatial discretizations. Since full discretization is essential for practical applications, it is vital to establish when it converges.
AI.method: The authors analyze convergence of a full discretization scheme using finite elements for spatial discretization and Lie splitting for temporal discretization of operator-valued differential Riccati equations.
AI.result: The method converges with order one in time and order two in space (except for logarithmic factors) under fairly weak assumptions on the problem data. Numerical experiments from optimal control applications support the theoretical findings.
AI.conclusion: This provides the first convergence analysis for full discretization of DRE, confirming that the combined scheme is reliable for practical use.

title: Solar Energetic Particle Reflection by Precursor ICMEs: Multi-spacecraft Observations of Bi-Directional Electron Beams at 1 AU
summary: We present case studies of two impulsive solar energetic electron (SEE) events during which particles at energies from 1-600 keV were detected by THEMIS-ARTEMIS orbiting the Moon, Wind at Earth's first Lagrange point, and (for one event) STEREO-A located at 1 AU, off the Sun-Earth line. The SEEs were initially highly anisotropic, traveling outward along the magnetic field with distinct energy-time dispersion. For one event, the spectra contained inverse velocity dispersion (IVD) signatures, whereby electrons at intermediate energies arrived to the spacecraft before those at higher energies. Similar features were recently discovered within 1 AU for energetic protons; this represents the first IVD detection for energetic electrons at Earth's orbital distance. During both events, a second beam of counter-streaming electrons was detected after a short time. Based on the time-delay in the detections at various energies, the path traveled by these counter-streaming electrons was on the order of 1-2 AU. We show that an interplanetary coronal mass ejection (ICME) passed the spacecraft a few days prior to the onset of each event and was located beyond 1 AU when the SEEs were detected, suggesting that the electrons were part of the same population, but reflected off the shock front of these precursor ICMEs. In the context of solar system exploration, this represents an unidentified hazard for astronaut safety beyond low-Earth orbit: although the initial phase of impulsive SEE events typically stream anti-Sunward, ICMEs located beyond Earth provide a mechanism for hazardous particles to travel Sunward during extreme events.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes two impulsive solar energetic electron events where 1-600 keV electrons were detected by multiple spacecraft. It reports the first detection of inverse velocity dispersion (IVD) for electrons at 1 AU, shows evidence that electrons reflected off ICME shock fronts located beyond 1 AU, and identifies this as a previously unrecognized radiation hazard for astronauts beyond low-Earth orbit.
AI.motivation: To understand the propagation mechanisms of solar energetic electrons and assess radiation hazards for deep-space exploration, particularly focusing on the unexpected Sunward-traveling electron populations.
AI.method: Case study analysis using multi-spacecraft observations (THEMIS-ARTEMIS at Moon, Wind at L1, STEREO-A at 1 AU) of two impulsive SEE events, examining energy-time dispersion, velocity dispersion signatures, and timing of counter-streaming electron beams.
AI.result: First detection of inverse velocity dispersion (IVD) for energetic electrons at 1 AU; counter-streaming electrons traveled 1-2 AU; precursor ICMEs located beyond 1 AU acted as mirrors reflecting electrons Sunward.
AI.conclusion: ICMEs beyond Earth can reflect solar energetic electrons back toward Earth, creating an unrecognized radiation hazard for astronauts in deep space beyond low-Earth orbit.

title: Higher order Schrödinger operators
summary: In this paper we consider higher order Schrödinger operators $$\mathcal L u=Lu+Vu,$$ where $L$ denotes a fourth order operator and $V\geq 0$ a suitable potential. We initiate our analysis by considering the constant coefficients differential operator $L=Δ^2$. Subsequently, we extend our results to more general operators $L$ featuring suitable variable coefficients. We are interested in domain characterization and generation properties of these operators in $L^p(\mathbb{R}^N)$ for $p \in (1, \infty)$. To address this problems we employ a noncommutative version of the Dore-Venni theorem due to Monniaux and Prüss and we prove that the $L^p$-realization of $\mathcal L$ is quasi sectorial and, consequently, generates an analytic semigroup. Furthermore, this approach allows for a sharp characterization of the operator's domain as the intersection of the domains of the bilaplacian and the multiplication operator. The required assumptions allow to treat potentials that grow at infinity like $|x|^r$ for some $r<4$.

AI.tldr: This paper studies higher order Schrödinger operators with fourth-order elliptic operators and nonnegative potentials, proving that their L^p-realization generates an analytic semigroup with a sharp domain characterization, using noncommutative Dore-Venni theory for potentials growing like |x|^r with r<4.
AI.motivation: To characterize the domain and generation properties of higher order Schrödinger operators in L^p spaces, extending results from second-order to fourth-order operators with variable coefficients.
AI.method: Uses a noncommutative version of the Dore-Venni theorem by Monniaux and Prüss to prove quasi-sectorial properties and analytic semigroup generation. Starts with constant coefficient case (Δ²) then extends to variable coefficient operators.
AI.result: The L^p-realization of the operator is quasi-sectorial and generates an analytic semigroup. The operator's domain is sharply characterized as the intersection of the domains of the bilaplacian and the multiplication operator, for potentials growing like |x|^r with r<4.
AI.conclusion: The approach successfully handles higher-order Schrödinger operators with variable coefficients and provides a sharp domain description, with admissible potentials growing at infinity at a rate less than quartic.

title: Excitation of Low-Frequency Modes and the Effects of Protein Dynamics on Spectral Densities of Bacteriochlorophyll Molecules
summary: In the theory of open quantum systems, spectral densities are key quantities for modeling the dynamics and spectroscopic properties of the system under investigation. In the case of light-harvesting complexes, they encode the frequency-dependent coupling of electronic excitations in pigment molecules to their environment, reflecting contributions from both intrinsic vibrational modes and the protein surrounding. In particular, the low-frequency components of the spectral densities are crucial for exciton transfer between pigment molecules. Apparently, slow internal modes of bacteriocholophyll molecules in the gas phase are less well represented by common force fields based on classical molecular dynamics (MD) simulations. Here, we demonstrate that Born-Oppenheimer molecular dynamics (BOMD) based on the numerically efficient density functional-based tight-binding approach can accurately recover these low-frequency features, whereas normal mode analysis captures them only partially. In contrasting approaches for determining spectral densities, the low-frequency region of the spectral densities obtained is only associated with protein fluctuations; the usage of BOMD, however, also captures the low-frequency contributions arising from slow intramolecular vibrations of the pigment molecules themselves. Notably, this behavior is consistently observed for both the flexible B800 and the more rigid B850 rings in light-harvesting 2 (LH2) complexes of purple bacteria, as well as in the Fenna-Matthews-Olson (FMO) complex of green sulfur bacteria. Interestingly, we also find that the spectral densities of the pigments in the B850 ring of LH2 are not influenced by the environment, i.e., the gaps between ground and first excited state are not changed significantly by the fluctuations of the protein environment.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that Born-Oppenheimer molecular dynamics (BOMD) based on density functional tight-binding can accurately capture low-frequency spectral density contributions from slow intramolecular vibrations of pigment molecules in light-harvesting complexes, which are missed by classical MD and normal mode analysis.
AI.motivation: Low-frequency components of spectral densities are crucial for exciton transfer between pigment molecules, but classical MD force fields poorly represent slow internal modes of bacteriochlorophyll molecules in the gas phase.
AI.method: The authors use Born-Oppenheimer molecular dynamics (BOMD) based on density functional-based tight-binding (DFTB) to compute spectral densities, comparing with normal mode analysis and classical MD approaches.
AI.result: BOMD accurately recovers low-frequency features missed by normal mode analysis and classical MD. Spectral densities of B850 ring pigments in LH2 are not significantly influenced by protein environment fluctuations.
AI.conclusion: BOMD captures low-frequency contributions from slow intramolecular vibrations of pigments themselves (not just protein fluctuations), consistently observed across multiple light-harvesting complexes.

title: Fractional calculus via variable-transform-based spectral approximations
summary: We present a novel and unifying framework for constructing spectral approximations to fractional integral operators. These spectral approximations are based on transplanted Chebyshev polynomials, which are obtained by composing Chebyshev polynomials with a variable transform. When an algebraic transform is used, the framework produces spectral approximations based on Jacobi fractional polynomials. When an exponential transform is used, it yields a versatile spectral approximation that is applicable to a much broader class of fractional calculus problems. The construction of such spectral approximations is both numerically stable and optimal in terms of complexity. These spectral approximations lead to stable and fast spectral methods for fractional calculus. The spectral approximation based on the double-exponential transform is demonstrated through extensive numerical examples that are intractable for existing spectral methods.

AI.tldr: A unifying framework for spectral approximations of fractional integral operators using transplanted Chebyshev polynomials, with algebraic transforms yielding Jacobi fractional polynomials and exponential transforms enabling broader applicability, resulting in stable, optimal-complexity spectral methods for fractional calculus.
AI.motivation: To develop a unifying framework for constructing spectral approximations to fractional integral operators that is numerically stable, optimal in complexity, and applicable to a broader class of fractional calculus problems (including intractable ones for existing spectral methods).
AI.method: The framework uses transplanted Chebyshev polynomials (Chebyshev polynomials composed with variable transforms). Algebraic transforms produce Jacobi fractional polynomials; exponential (double-exponential) transforms produce a more versatile spectral approximation.
AI.result: The construction is numerically stable and optimal in complexity. The double-exponential transform-based spectral approximation successfully handles extensive numerical examples that are intractable for existing spectral methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides stable, fast, and broadly applicable spectral methods for fractional calculus, significantly extending the capabilities beyond existing spectral approaches.

title: Rotation Measure Substructures Induced by the Ponderomotive Force of Inertial \alfven Waves
summary: The rotation measure (RM) and dispersion measure (DM) of fast radio bursts (FRBs) serve as critical probes of the magneto-ionic environments along the line of sight. The significant temporal evolution of RM observed in some repeating FRBs is generally attributed to the local environment of the source, since the intergalactic medium is not expected to vary on such short timescales. Recent observations of repeating FRB 20201124A and FRB 20220529 exhibit complex RM phenomenology, including large-amplitude global fluctuations and short-term substructures. Here, we attribute these short-term RM variations to the ponderomotive force exerted by inertial \alfven~waves (IAWs). We propose that IAWs, generated via magnetic reconnection or turbulent cascades in a low-$β$ plasma, induce nonlinear density perturbations in the source environment. We demonstrate that the resulting plasma density redistribution can produce RM suppression consistent with observed substructures. This model presents a physically motivated mechanism for the short-term RM variability observed in active repeaters. It demonstrates that such fluctuations can arise from wave-driven density cavitation within a broad, coupled parameter space involving wave amplitude, plasma density, and temperature, thereby characterizing the localized plasma dynamics required to produce the observed RM jitters.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes that short-term RM variations in repeating FRBs (like FRB 20201124A and FRB 20220529) are caused by nonlinear density perturbations from inertial Alfvén waves (IAWs) generated via magnetic reconnection or turbulent cascades in low-β plasma, providing a physically motivated mechanism for observed RM jitters.
AI.motivation: To explain the observed short-term RM substructures and variability in repeating FRBs, which cannot be attributed to intergalactic medium variations and lack a well-understood physical mechanism.
AI.method: The authors propose that inertial Alfvén waves (IAWs) generate nonlinear density perturbations via ponderomotive force in low-β plasma, leading to density redistribution (cavitation) that suppresses RM, and they explore the coupled parameter space of wave amplitude, plasma density, and temperature.
AI.result: The model demonstrates that RM suppression consistent with observed substructures can arise from IAW-driven density cavitation within a broad, coupled parameter space.
AI.conclusion: IAW-driven density perturbations offer a physically motivated mechanism for short-term RM variability in active repeaters, characterizing the localized plasma dynamics responsible for RM jitters.

title: Sharp Strichartz estimate for the 1D periodic Schrödinger equation
summary: We prove the following estimate \[ \|{e^{it\partial_x^2}f}\|_{L_{(t,x)\in \mathbb{T}^2}^6}\leq C (\log N)^{1/6} \|f\|_{L^2_x(\mathbb{T})}, \] assuming $\mbox{supp} (\hat f)\subset [-N,N]$ for $N>1$. The bound $(\log N)^{1/6}$ is sharp in view of the lower bound by Bourgain \cite{Bourgain}.

AI.tldr: This paper proves a sharp (log N)^{1/6} bound for the L^6 norm of the linear Schrödinger equation on a torus, matching Bourgain's lower bound.
AI.motivation: To establish sharp estimates for the Strichartz norm of the Schrödinger equation on the torus, improving upon prior results and matching known lower bounds.
AI.method: Analytic estimates on the linear Schrödinger propagator e^{it∂²_x} with band-limited initial data (supp(ˆf) ⊂ [-N,N]), proving an L⁶ norm bound of order (log N)^{1/6}.
AI.result: Proved ‖e^{it∂²_x} f‖_{L⁶(T²)} ≤ C (log N)^{1/6} ‖f‖_{L²(T)}, which is sharp up to constant factor.
AI.conclusion: The log N growth is optimal, as it matches Bourgain's lower bound, settling the sharpness of the estimate.

title: Jarvis-HEP: A lightweight Python framework for workflow composition and parameter scans in high-energy physics
summary: High-energy physics phenomenology often requires linking multiple computational tools to evaluate observables, likelihoods, and experimental constraints across nontrivial parameter spaces. In this work, we introduce Jarvis-HEP, a lightweight Python framework for workflow composition and parameter scans in high-energy physics. The framework provides YAML-based workflow specification, dependency-aware execution, modular calculator integration, and asynchronous task scheduling for multi-step computational studies. It supports both external software packages and internally implemented components within a unified workflow, and the current implementation includes several built-in sampling backends for exploratory scans. This paper describes the design and user interface of Jarvis-HEP and illustrates its use with representative synthetic and phenomenological examples.

AI.tldr: Jarvis-HEP is a lightweight Python framework for workflow composition and parameter scans in high-energy physics phenomenology, featuring YAML-based specification, dependency-aware execution, modular calculators, and asynchronous task scheduling.
AI.motivation: High-energy physics phenomenology requires linking multiple computational tools to evaluate observables, likelihoods, and experimental constraints across nontrivial parameter spaces, creating a need for unified workflow management.
AI.method: The framework provides YAML-based workflow specification, dependency-aware execution, modular calculator integration, asynchronous task scheduling, supports both external and internal components, and includes built-in sampling backends.
AI.result: The paper describes the design and user interface of Jarvis-HEP and illustrates its use with representative synthetic and phenomenological examples.
AI.conclusion: Jarvis-HEP offers a lightweight, modular solution for workflow composition and parameter scans in high-energy physics, enabling efficient multi-step computational studies.

title: A Discrete-Time Random Feature Method for Nonlinear Evolution Equations with Implicit-Explicit Runge--Kutta Time Stepping
summary: We study a discrete-time random feature method for nonlinear, time-dependent partial differential equations. In contrast to continuous-time formulations that treat time as an additional input variable, the method advances the solution step by step, with each time level computed from previously available states. The spatial solution at each step is represented in the random feature trial space, and the time discretization is given by an implicit-explicit Runge--Kutta (IMEX-RK, 4 stages, third-order) scheme. After splitting the operator into linear and nonlinear parts, each stage admits a linear least-squares formulation, which avoids nonlinear least-squares solves. We also derive a global error estimate for the fully discrete method, separating the contributions of the stage-wise RFM approximation, perturbations in the least-squares coefficients, and the temporal discretization. Numerical experiments for the Allen--Cahn, Burgers, Korteweg--De Vries, and Cahn--Hilliard equations show relative $L^2$-errors of order $10^{-6}$ and convergence rates consistent with the third-order IMEX scheme. A comparison with an IMEX-PINN variant shows that the proposed method achieves higher accuracy at substantially lower computational cost.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a discrete-time random feature method for solving nonlinear time-dependent PDEs, using IMEX-RK time stepping and linear least-squares solves, achieving high accuracy (10⁻⁶ relative L² errors) and third-order convergence at much lower computational cost than IMEX-PINNs.
AI.motivation: To develop a more efficient and accurate alternative to continuous-time random feature methods and PINN-based approaches for solving nonlinear time-dependent PDEs, avoiding costly nonlinear least-squares solves.
AI.method: A discrete-time random feature method combining IMEX-RK (4-stage, 3rd-order) time discretization with random feature spatial representation, splitting the operator into linear/nonlinear parts to enable linear least-squares formulation per stage.
AI.result: Relative L²-errors around 10⁻⁶, convergence rates consistent with third-order IMEX scheme on Allen-Cahn, Burgers, KdV, and Cahn-Hilliard equations; significantly outperforms IMEX-PINN in accuracy and computational cost.
AI.conclusion: The discrete-time RFM with IMEX-RK provides a robust, high-accuracy, and computationally efficient framework for nonlinear time-dependent PDEs, with theoretical error estimates validated by numerical experiments.

title: Boundary epsilon regularity for incompressible Navier--Stokes equations via weak-strong uniqueness
summary: We show that finite-energy weak solutions to the incompressible Navier--Stokes equations on a three-dimensional bounded smooth domain are regular up to the boundary, provided that the $L^4_tL^4_x$-norm of the solution is smaller than a constant depending only on the domain. This answers a problem raised in [D. Albritton, T. Barker, and C. Prange, J. Math. Fluid Mech. 25 (2023), Paper No. 49]. Our proof relies on a new slicing construction near the boundary of the domain.

AI.tldr: This paper proves that weak solutions to 3D Navier-Stokes equations on bounded smooth domains are regular up to the boundary if their L^4_tL^4_x norm is below a domain-dependent constant, using a new boundary slicing technique.
AI.motivation: To resolve an open problem raised by Albritton, Barker, and Prange (2023) regarding regularity criteria for weak solutions of the Navier-Stokes equations near boundaries, which is essential for understanding the boundary behavior of fluid flows.
AI.method: The proof introduces a novel slicing construction near the boundary of the domain, enabling control of solutions up to the boundary under the L^4_tL^4_x norm condition.
AI.result: Finite-energy weak solutions are regular up to the boundary when the L^4_tL^4_x norm is bounded by a constant that depends only on the domain geometry.
AI.conclusion: A sufficient condition (L^4_tL^4_x norm below domain-dependent threshold) ensures full boundary regularity, answering the open question from prior work.

title: Numerical Investigations of Stable Dynamics in the Presence of Ghosts
comment: 19 pages (including 12 figures and 19 tables) with 2 appendices and references
summary: We explore the nonlinear dynamics of classical field theories containing ghost degrees of freedom, focusing on two coupled scalar fields with opposite kinetic terms in (1+1) and (2+1) dimensional Minkowski spacetime. Using a spacetime finite element formulation, we perform a systematic numerical study across a broad class of initial data. We find that ghost-normal systems can exhibit long-lived, dynamically bounded evolution over extended time intervals, with stability strongly controlled by spectral content and amplitude. Ultraviolet-dominated and small-amplitude configurations remain stable significantly longer than infrared-dominated or large-amplitude data, indicating that instability is mediated by nonlinear spectral energy transfer rather than instantaneous runaway. Nonlinear self-interactions play a dual role: while they can accelerate energy exchange between sectors, certain potentials, including a lifted $φ^6$ interaction supporting oscillon-like structures, generate transient metastable regimes that partially suppress ghost-induced growth. Our results demonstrate that the dynamical consequences of ghost modes in classical field theory depend sensitively on dispersion, nonlinearity, and phase structure, revealing a richer metastability landscape than commonly assumed.

AI.tldr: This paper numerically studies two coupled scalar fields with opposite kinetic terms (ghost-normal system) in (1+1) and (2+1) dimensions, finding that ghost-induced instability is mediated by nonlinear spectral energy transfer rather than instantaneous runaway, and that certain potentials can create transient metastable regimes that partially suppress ghost growth.
AI.motivation: To understand the nonlinear dynamics and stability of classical field theories containing ghost degrees of freedom, challenging the common assumption that ghosts lead to immediate catastrophic instability.
AI.method: Using a spacetime finite element formulation, the authors perform systematic numerical simulations of two coupled scalar fields with opposite kinetic terms across various initial data in (1+1) and (2+1) dimensional Minkowski spacetime.
AI.result: Ghost-normal systems can exhibit long-lived, dynamically bounded evolution. Stability is strongly controlled by spectral content and amplitude: UV-dominated and small-amplitude configurations remain stable significantly longer. Nonlinear self-interactions play a dual role in accelerating or suppressing energy exchange.
AI.conclusion: The dynamical consequences of ghost modes depend sensitively on dispersion, nonlinearity, and phase structure, revealing a richer metastability landscape than commonly assumed.

title: A sharp-interface model for solid-state dewetting with wetting potential
summary: We propose a sharp-interface model for solid-state dewetting of thin films with wetting potential, where the wetting effect is incorporated through a thickness-dependent surface energy. The model is governed by surface diffusion together with natural boundary conditions, and describes the morphological evolution of the film-vapor interface. For its numerical approximation, we develop an efficient semi-implicit finite element method based on a Taylor expansion of the wetting-potential term. Numerical simulations in two dimensions show that the proposed model and method can capture various dewetting phenomena. They also indicate that, as the range of the wetting potential tends to zero, the proposed model approaches the sharp-interface model with thickness-independent surface energy proposed in [1]. The model and numerical method are further extended to three dimensions, where the computations capture complex morphological evolution in solid-state dewetting.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a sharp-interface model for solid-state dewetting of thin films that includes wetting potential via thickness-dependent surface energy, develops an efficient semi-implicit finite element method for numerical approximation, and extends the model and method to 3D to capture complex dewetting phenomena.
AI.motivation: To better model solid-state dewetting of thin films by incorporating wetting potential effects through a thickness-dependent surface energy, which is more realistic than previous thickness-independent models.
AI.method: The model uses surface diffusion with natural boundary conditions and a Taylor expansion of the wetting-potential term for efficient semi-implicit finite element numerical approximation, extended from 2D to 3D.
AI.result: Numerical simulations in 2D capture various dewetting phenomena, and as wetting potential range approaches zero, the model converges to the thickness-independent model. 3D computations capture complex morphological evolution.
AI.conclusion: The proposed model and numerical method effectively simulate solid-state dewetting with wetting effects in both 2D and 3D, approaching prior models in limiting cases.

title: On nonnegative solutions of the parabolic differential inequality with $(p,q)$-Laplace on Riemannian manifolds
summary: In this paper, we establish Liouville-type theorems for parabolic differential inequalities with $(p,q)-$Laplacian operator on Riemannian manifolds. By a test function argument, we establish nonexistence results under suitable weighted volume growth assumptions involving potential. In particular, we can obtain nonexistence results for a wider class of parabolic inequalities.

AI.tldr: This paper proves Liouville-type theorems (showing only trivial solutions exist) for parabolic differential inequalities with the (p,q)-Laplacian operator on Riemannian manifolds, using test function arguments and weighted volume growth assumptions.
AI.motivation: To establish nonexistence results for parabolic inequalities with (p,q)-Laplacian on manifolds, extending existing theory to a broader class of problems.
AI.method: Test function argument combined with weighted volume growth assumptions involving potential on Riemannian manifolds.
AI.result: Obtained Liouville-type theorems (nonexistence of non-trivial solutions) under suitable weighted volume growth conditions.
AI.conclusion: The approach yields nonexistence results for a wider class of parabolic inequalities than previously possible.

title: Spin-Axis-Layer Locking for Intrinsic Bipolar Altermagnetic Semiconductors: Proof-of-Concept in Bilayer CuBr2
summary: Electrical control of spin and magnetic sublattice degrees of freedom is essential for multifunctional and low-power spintronic devices. Bipolar altermagnetic semiconductors (BAMSs)-characterized by opposite spin polarizations at the valence and conduction band edges-offer such control, yet known systems require external strain and sizable valley polarization for gate-tunable switching. Here, we propose a universal spin-axis-layer locking (SALL) paradigm to overcome these limitations. By stacking two quasi-1D ferromagnetic monolayers with a 90 degrees twist, the bilayer reconstructs altermagnetic symmetry, yielding an intrinsic BAMS state where carrier spin is locked to specific layers and transport directions. Using synthesized CuBr2 monolayers as proof-of-concept, we demonstrate via first-principles calculations a robust BAMS state. Electrostatic gating enables simultaneous, reversible switching of carrier type, spin, and active layer, generating fully spin-polarized axial charge currents and directionally controllable pure spin currents with near-unity charge-to-spin conversion efficiency. This SALL model establishes a versatile, strain-independent strategy for advanced all-electrical altermagnetic devices.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a "spin-axis-layer locking" (SALL) paradigm using twisted quasi-1D ferromagnetic monolayers to create intrinsic bipolar altermagnetic semiconductors (BAMS), enabling full electrical control of spin and layer degrees of freedom without external strain.
AI.motivation: To achieve electrical control of spin and magnetic sublattice degrees of freedom for multifunctional, low-power spintronic devices; existing BAMS systems require external strain and sizable valley polarization for gate-tunable switching, which limits practical applications.
AI.method: Proposes a universal spin-axis-layer locking (SALL) paradigm by stacking two quasi-1D ferromagnetic monolayers with a 90° twist. Uses synthesized CuBr2 monolayers as proof-of-concept with first-principles calculations to demonstrate the BAMS state.
AI.result: Demonstrates a robust intrinsic BAMS state in twisted CuBr2 bilayers where carrier spin is locked to specific layers and transport directions. Electrostatic gating enables reversible switching of carrier type, spin, and active layer, generating fully spin-polarized axial charge currents and directionally controllable pure spin currents with near-unity charge-to-spin conversion efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The SALL model provides a versatile, strain-independent strategy for advanced all-electrical altermagnetic devices, overcoming limitations of previous BAMS systems.

title: A quantitative direct sampling method for inhomogeneities from multi-frequency backscattering measurements
summary: The inverse scattering problem from the multi-frequency backscattering data is a long-standing open problem. We advance the theory by proving a local uniqueness result. Moreover, we introduce a direct sampling method for quantitatively reconstructing unknown inhomogeneities. Comprehensive numerical experiments validate the robustness, accuracy, and computational effectiveness of the proposed quantitative direct sampling method.

AI.tldr: This paper advances inverse scattering theory by proving local uniqueness for multi-frequency backscattering data, and introduces a direct sampling method for quantitative reconstruction of unknown inhomogeneities, validated through numerical experiments.
AI.motivation: The inverse scattering problem from multi-frequency backscattering data has been a long-standing open problem, and the authors aim to address this theoretical gap while developing practical reconstruction methods.
AI.method: The authors prove a local uniqueness result for the inverse scattering problem using multi-frequency backscattering data, and propose a direct sampling method for quantitatively reconstructing unknown inhomogeneities.
AI.result: The proposed quantitative direct sampling method demonstrates robustness, accuracy, and computational effectiveness through comprehensive numerical experiments.
AI.conclusion: The work advances both theoretical understanding (local uniqueness) and practical methodology (direct sampling) for inverse scattering problems with multi-frequency backscattering data.

title: Entropic regularization of Monge's problem
comment: v2 fixes a compilation issue of cleveref
summary: We study the vanishing-regularization limit of entropically regularized optimal transport (EOT) for the Euclidean distance cost $c(x,y)=\|x-y\|$ in dimension $d>1$. We develop a comprehensive variational convergence framework that entails two main results. First, we resolve the longstanding entropic selection problem: the EOT minimizer converges to a distinguished optimal transport plan that is characterized explicitly as the solution of a constrained EOT problem on each transport ray. Denoting by $\varepsilon>0$ the regularization parameter, this selection holds for all $o(\varepsilon)$-approximate minimizers, with sharp failure at the $O(\varepsilon)$ scale. Second, we establish an explicit second-order expansion of the entropic transport cost. The second-order term encodes the geometry of the regularization and reveals the optimal asymptotic tradeoff between entropy and transport cost.

AI.tldr: This paper studies the limit as regularization vanishes in entropic optimal transport (EOT) with Euclidean distance cost. It resolves the entropic selection problem (minimizers converge to a distinguished transport plan on each transport ray) and provides a second-order expansion of the transport cost revealing the geometry-regularization tradeoff.
AI.motivation: To understand the behavior of entropically regularized optimal transport as the regularization parameter ε → 0, and to resolve the longstanding question of which optimal transport plan is selected in the vanishing-regularization limit.
AI.method: A variational convergence framework is developed to analyze the EOT minimizer limit. The analysis distinguishes between o(ε)-approximate minimizers (convergence holds) and O(ε)-scale (sharp failure), and derives the second-order asymptotic expansion of the entropic transport cost.
AI.result: 1) The EOT minimizer converges to a distinguished optimal transport plan characterized by a constrained EOT problem on each transport ray. 2) This selection holds for all o(ε)-approximate minimizers but fails sharply at the O(ε) scale. 3) An explicit second-order expansion of the entropic transport cost is provided, where the second-order term captures the geometry of regularization.
AI.conclusion: The work resolves the entropic selection problem for EOT with Euclidean cost in d>1, and reveals the optimal asymptotic tradeoff between entropy and transport cost through the second-order expansion.

title: Validity of DFT+U band gaps in all its known functional forms
summary: The Density Functional Theory plus Hubbard $U$ (DFT+$U$) technique is one of the most widely used tools by condensed matter physicists and solid state chemists for the simulation of transition-metal and lanthanide bearing crystals, and increasingly of much more diverse chemistries. Although often synonymous with the corrective functionals of Dudarev et al. and Liechtenstein et al., there exists a wide variety of DFT+$U$-type functionals ready to be utilized, and no doubt yet to be developed. Since the earliest days, the gap in the DFT+$U$ single-particle eigenspectrum has been associated with the fundamental band gap, and the method has typically found more success for spectra than for total-energy derived properties. There has been some doubt, however, as to the conceptual validity of this association. Here, extending findings from recent years regarding local and semi-local functionals, we prove that the DFT+$U$ eigenspectrum gap is indeed valid, in the sense that it matches its own fundamental gap calculated using total-energy differences. This is true for pristine periodic systems with converged $k$-point sampling but not, however, for defective ones or isolated systems. We show that bandgap validity for solids holds in the presence of pseudopotentials and PAW potentials, when using hybrid functionals, and in DFT+$U$(+$J$) irrespective of the level of subspace projection onto the band-edge states. We survey every DFT+$U$-type functional known to have been published to date, within a unified notation. We verify analytically under which conditions the eigenvalue gap equals its fundamental gap for each functional, and analyze its effect on total energies and gaps for the hydrogen lattice in the Mott-Hubbard limit.

AI.tldr: This paper proves that the DFT+U eigenspectrum gap is valid for pristine periodic systems (matching the fundamental gap from total-energy differences), but not for defective or isolated systems. It surveys all known DFT+U-type functionals within a unified notation and verifies the conditions for gap equality.
AI.motivation: To resolve conceptual doubts about whether the DFT+U single-particle eigenspectrum gap can be validly associated with the fundamental band gap, and to provide a comprehensive, unified survey of all DFT+U-type functionals published to date.
AI.method: Extending recent findings on local/semi-local functionals, the authors analytically prove the equivalence of the DFT+U eigenvalue gap and the fundamental gap (via total-energy differences) for pristine periodic systems. They survey all published DFT+U-type functionals under a unified notation and verify conditions for gap equality analytically, as well as test effects on the hydrogen lattice in the Mott-Hubbard limit.
AI.result: The DFT+U eigenspectrum gap is valid for pristine periodic systems with converged k-point sampling, even with pseudopotentials, PAW potentials, hybrid functionals, and regardless of subspace projection level. However, it is not valid for defective or isolated systems.
AI.conclusion: DFT+U eigenspectrum gaps can be reliably interpreted as fundamental band gaps for pristine periodic solids, but caution is needed for defective or isolated systems where the association breaks down.

title: C-PINN: A neural network framework based on the Cordès condition for solving linear and fully nonlinear equations in non-divergence form and its applications
summary: In this paper, we propose a novel Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) framework based on the Cordès condition for solving both linear and fully nonlinear partial differential equations (PDEs) in non-divergence form, together with their applications. By incorporating the operator structure into the loss function, the proposed method improves the conditioning of the associated optimization problem, thereby enhancing training stability and solution accuracy. The framework is further extended to include Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman and Monge-Ampère equations, with applications to optimal transport. Numerical experiments demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of the method, as well as its capability to address high-dimensional problems, highlighting the promise of learning-based approaches for tackling challenging PDEs. Owing to its generality and simplicity, the proposed method is expected to be of broad interest to the scientific and engineering communities.

AI.tldr: A PINN framework using the Cordès condition improves training stability and accuracy for solving linear and fully nonlinear PDEs, including Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman and Monge-Ampère equations, with applications to optimal transport.
AI.motivation: To improve training stability and solution accuracy of PINNs for non-divergence form PDEs by incorporating the Cordès condition into the loss function to better condition the optimization problem.
AI.method: A PINN framework that integrates the Cordès condition-based operator structure into the loss function, extending to Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman and Monge-Ampère equations for optimal transport applications.
AI.result: Numerical experiments demonstrate effectiveness, robustness, and capability to handle high-dimensional problems.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method is general, simple, and promising for scientific and engineering communities addressing challenging PDEs.

title: Backstepping Observer for the Quasilinear Heat Equation with Linear Design Gains: Beyond Local Stability
comment: This is a working document of a work in progress
summary: We consider the one-dimensional quasilinear heat equation with state-dependent heat capacity and thermal conductivity, and design a boundary-output observer based on the backstepping design for a linear heat equation with constant coefficients. Viewing the quasilinear system as a perturbation of the linear one, we establish exponential stability of the origin for the observation error dynamics in $H^1$, with an explicit region of attraction depending on the system parameters, observer gains, and the mismatch between the nonlinear diffusivity and the constant design diffusivity. Importantly, the observation error converges to zero rather than merely to a neighborhood scaling with this mismatch, even though, in contrast to backstepping-based stabilization of nonlinear PDEs, the mismatch need not decay along trajectories and may remain bounded away from zero, acting as a persistent state-dependent multiplicative perturbation. A technical challenge was to perform a sufficiently-fine Lyapunov analysis that does not yield overly conservative results such as mere boundedness of the observation error. Interestingly, while in the linear case the relationship between one of the backstepping observer gains and the convergence rate is monotonic, we show that in the nonlinear setting this is no longer the case: there may exist an optimal value of that gain, beyond which further increases deteriorate the system's performance. Such behavior cannot be predicted without our analysis: one might expect a priori the decay rate to be freely tunable at the expense of a region of attraction that shrinks to zero as the prescribed rate tends to infinity. However, our Lyapunov analysis (supported by numerical experiments) reveals that this intuition is incorrect.

AI.tldr: This paper designs a backstepping-based boundary-output observer for a 1D quasilinear heat equation with state-dependent coefficients, treating it as a perturbation of a linear system. The authors prove exponential stability of the observation error in H^1 with an explicit region of attraction, and surprisingly show that the observer gain has an optimal value—beyond which performance degrades—contrary to the linear case intuition.
AI.motivation: To develop an observer for quasilinear heat equations with state-dependent heat capacity and thermal conductivity, where the nonlinearity acts as a persistent state-dependent perturbation that does not vanish along trajectories, making standard backstepping approaches for nonlinear PDEs insufficient.
AI.method: The observer is designed via backstepping applied to a linear heat equation with constant coefficients. The quasilinear system is viewed as a perturbation of this linear system. A Lyapunov analysis is performed to prove exponential stability of the observation error, with careful treatment to avoid overly conservative boundedness results.
AI.result: Exponential stability of the observation error in H^1 norm is established with an explicit region of attraction. The error converges to zero despite persistent mismatch. Counterintuitively, the observer gain has an optimal value beyond which performance worsens—a non-monotonic relationship not present in the linear case.
AI.conclusion: The analysis reveals that the intuitive expectation of freely tunable decay rate at the expense of a shrinking region of attraction is incorrect. The non-monotonic gain-performance relationship is a genuinely nonlinear phenomenon that cannot be predicted from linear theory.

title: Quantum-Inspired Robust and Scalable SAR Object Classification
summary: SAR image classification naturally has to deal with huge noise and a high dynamic range particularly requiring robust classification models. Additionally, the deployment of these models on edge devices, such as drones and military aircraft, requires a careful balance between model size and classification accuracy. This study explores the potential of tensor networks to meet these robustness requirements, specifically evaluating their resilience to data poisoning. Unlike previous works that concentrated on conventional neural networks for SAR object detection, this research focuses on the robustness and model reduction capabilities of tensor networks in object classification. Our findings indicate that tensor networks are adept at addressing both the challenges of robustness and the need for model efficiency, thereby contributing valuable insights to the ongoing discourse in radar applications and deep learning methodologies in general.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates tensor networks for SAR image classification, showing they can achieve both robustness to noise/data poisoning and model efficiency suitable for edge deployment.
AI.motivation: SAR images have huge noise and high dynamic range requiring robust models, and edge deployment (drones, military aircraft) demands a balance between model size and accuracy.
AI.method: The study evaluates tensor networks' resilience to data poisoning and their model reduction capabilities for SAR object classification, rather than using conventional neural networks.
AI.result: Tensor networks effectively address both robustness challenges and model efficiency requirements.
AI.conclusion: Tensor networks provide valuable insights for radar applications and deep learning by balancing robustness and model size for edge deployment.

title: A bound-preserving oscillation-eliminating discontinuous Galerkin scheme for compressible two-phase flow
summary: This paper presents a high-order bound-preserving oscillation-eliminating discontinuous Galerkin (BP-OEDG) scheme for simulating gas-gas and gas-liquid two-phase flows governed by the Kapila five-equation model with the Tammann equation of state (EOS). The primary computational bottleneck arises from the severe CFL restriction imposed by the stiff $κ$-source term in the volume fraction equation. To circumvent this, we propose a novel operator-splitting strategy that decouples the system into a transport model and a stiff $κ$-source term. The former is discretized via a quasi-conservative DG method \cite{cheng2020quasi}, while the latter is resolved by an adaptive implicit strategy hybridizing the backward Euler and SDIRK2 methods. We rigorously prove that this implicit treatment is unconditionally BP, effectively removing the stiffness-induced stability constraints inherent in traditional explicit schemes. To further enhance precision, a velocity divergence reconstruction inspired by the Local Discontinuous Galerkin (LDG) method is integrated into the implicit solver. Furthermore, an OE limiter is employed to suppress spurious oscillations without characteristic decomposition, complemented by a BP limiter to ensure the BP property of partial densities, pressure, and volume fraction. Crucially, we prove that the proposed BP-OEDG scheme, integrated with the splitting strategy, strictly satisfies the Abgrall condition. Extensive numerical experiments, including challenging water-air shock-bubble interactions, demonstrate the superior robustness and efficiency of the method.

AI.tldr: A high-order bound-preserving oscillation-eliminating DG scheme with operator-splitting and implicit stiff source term treatment is proposed for two-phase Kapila model flows, proven to satisfy the Abgrall condition and demonstrated via water-air shock-bubble interactions.
AI.motivation: The severe CFL restriction caused by the stiff κ-source term in the Kapila five-equation model creates a computational bottleneck that traditional explicit schemes cannot efficiently overcome.
AI.method: An operator-splitting strategy decouples the system into a transport model (solved via quasi-conservative DG) and a stiff κ-source term (solved via hybrid implicit backward Euler/SDIRK2). An LDG-inspired velocity divergence reconstruction enhances accuracy, while OE and BP limiters ensure stability and bound preservation.
AI.result: The implicit treatment is proven unconditionally BP, the scheme strictly satisfies the Abgrall condition, and numerical experiments (including water-air shock-bubble interactions) confirm superior robustness and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The proposed BP-OEDG scheme effectively removes stiffness-induced stability constraints while maintaining high-order accuracy, bound preservation, and oscillation control for two-phase flow simulations.

title: Non-uniqueness of nonlinear Markov processes in the sense of McKean associated with parabolic PDEs
summary: We derive a general scheme to construct infinitely many probabilistic counterparts for solutions to nonlinear PDEs by recasting the latter as different nonlinear Fokker--Planck equations and by constructing, for each of these equations, a solution to the associated McKean--Vlasov SDE with one-dimensional time marginal densities given by the PDE solution. We utilize this scheme to prove that nonlinear Markov processes in the sense of McKean as introduced by Rehmeier--Röckner (J.\,Theor.\,Probab. 38, 60 (2025)) are not uniquely determined by their one-dimensional time marginals. This is in sharp contrast to the case of classical Markov processes, which are uniquely determined by their one-dimensional time marginals. We demonstrate our results by constructing a continuum of nonlinear Markov processes with one-dimensional time marginal densities given by the Barenblatt solutions to the porous medium and $p$-Laplace equations, as well as by the fundamental solution to the heat equation. This includes a novel martingale representation for the $p$-Laplace Barenblatt solutions. We also prove that a nonlinear Markov process is uniquely determined by its two-dimensional time marginals. Moreover, for the porous medium equation, we show that the different McKean--Vlasov SDEs we investigate are consistent with corresponding gradient flow interpretations of the equation in the sense of Otto calculus.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that nonlinear Markov processes (McKean sense) are not uniquely determined by their one-dimensional time marginals, unlike classical Markov processes, by constructing infinitely many probabilistic counterparts for solutions to nonlinear PDEs via Fokker-Planck and McKean-Vlasov SDEs. Examples include porous medium, p-Laplace, and heat equations.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether nonlinear Markov processes are uniquely determined by their one-dimensional time marginals (as classical Markov processes are), and to develop a general scheme connecting nonlinear PDEs to probabilistic representations through Fokker-Planck equations and McKean-Vlasov SDEs.
AI.method: A general scheme is derived to construct infinitely many probabilistic counterparts for nonlinear PDE solutions by recasting them as nonlinear Fokker-Planck equations and constructing associated McKean-Vlasov SDEs with one-dimensional time marginal densities matching the PDE solution.
AI.result: Nonlinear Markov processes are NOT uniquely determined by their one-dimensional time marginals (contrasting classical Markov processes). A continuum of nonlinear Markov processes is constructed for Barenblatt solutions (porous medium, p-Laplace) and heat equation. A novel martingale representation for p-Laplace Barenblatt solutions is found. Nonlinear Markov processes are uniquely determined by two-dimensional time marginals.
AI.conclusion: The scheme successfully generates infinitely many probabilistic representations for nonlinear PDEs, demonstrating fundamental non-uniqueness of nonlinear Markov processes from 1D marginals. The constructed McKean-Vlasov SDEs for the porous medium equation are consistent with Otto calculus gradient flow interpretations.

title: Predicting challenging phase transitions with Bayesian active learning
comment: 10 main pages, 3 main figures, 6 supplementary pages, 9 supplementary figures
summary: Materials underpin modern technologies, from energy harvesting, storage, and conversion to information and communication technologies. Their functionality is often governed by the interplay between competing phases, as thermodynamic behavior shapes microscopic properties and ultimately determines technological performance; for instance, the light absorption of inorganic metal-halide perovskites in solar cells. Accurately predicting crystal thermodynamics, however, remains a major challenge for computational approaches because strong anharmonic effects require extensive sampling of the potential energy surface. Here, we present an on-the-fly Bayesian framework, combined with the stochastic self-consistent harmonic approximation, for learning first-principles interatomic potentials. This approach enables the prediction of thermodynamic properties over a broad temperature range with first-principles accuracy while requiring training on only a few tens to a few hundreds of atomic configurations. To demonstrate its power, we investigate the thermodynamic and dynamical properties of Li$_2$O, $α$-CsPbI$_3$, and $δ$-CsPbI$_3$, requiring only 44, 256, and 50 total-energy calculations, respectively. Notably, we show that this framework accurately captures the phase diagram of CsPbI$_3$, which explains its spontaneous degradation into the non-absorbing yellow phase, predicting the transition temperature with remarkable accuracy and efficiency. More broadly, the method presented opens a novel route toward accelerated materials engineering under realistic conditions for a wide range of technologically relevant applications, including solid-state batteries, optoelectronic devices, and memristors.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a Bayesian on-the-fly learning framework combined with stochastic self-consistent harmonic approximation to predict crystal thermodynamics from first-principles with minimal training data (tens to hundreds of configurations), accurately capturing phase diagrams and transition temperatures of materials like Li₂O and CsPbI₃.
AI.motivation: Accurately predicting crystal thermodynamics is a major computational challenge due to strong anharmonic effects requiring extensive sampling of the potential energy surface, yet understanding thermodynamic behavior is crucial for materials performance in applications like solar cells, batteries, and optoelectronics.
AI.method: An on-the-fly Bayesian framework combined with the stochastic self-consistent harmonic approximation (SSCHA) for learning first-principles interatomic potentials, requiring only tens to hundreds of atomic configurations for training.
AI.result: The method predicted thermodynamic properties of Li₂O (44 calculations), α-CsPbI₃ (256 calculations), and δ-CsPbI₃ (50 calculations) with first-principles accuracy, and accurately captured the CsPbI₃ phase diagram including the spontaneous degradation transition temperature into the non-absorbing yellow phase.
AI.conclusion: This framework opens a novel route toward accelerated materials engineering under realistic conditions for a wide range of applications including solid-state batteries, optoelectronic devices, and memristors.

title: Splitting AVF method for generalized Langevin equations: probability density function and geometric ergodicity
summary: The generalized Langevin equation (GLE) constitutes a fundamental model for describing nonequilibrium dynamics with memory effects. To overcome the numerical challenges arising from superquadratically growing potentials and degenerate noise, we propose and analyze a structure-preserving splitting averaged vector field (AVF) method for a quasi-Markovian GLE. The core advantage of this method lies in its ability to simultaneously preserve the exponential integrability, Malliavin differentiability, and ergodicity of the underlying continuous system. Notably, by leveraging exponential integrability, Malliavin differentiability, and uniform non-degeneracy of the numerical solution, we obtain the existence and smoothness of its probability density function, which converges to that of the exact solution with first-order accuracy. Furthermore, by validating the Lyapunov condition and the minorization condition using a localized technique, we establish the geometric ergodicity of the numerical solution. Finally, numerical experiments are conducted to confirm the theoretical results.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a structure-preserving splitting AVF method for the quasi-Markovian GLE that preserves exponential integrability, Malliavin differentiability, and ergodicity. It proves first-order convergence of the probability density function and geometric ergodicity of the numerical solution.
AI.motivation: To overcome numerical challenges arising from superquadratically growing potentials and degenerate noise in the generalized Langevin equation (GLE) with memory effects.
AI.method: A structure-preserving splitting averaged vector field (AVF) method for quasi-Markovian GLE.
AI.result: The method preserves exponential integrability, Malliavin differentiability, and ergodicity. The probability density function exists, is smooth, and converges to the exact solution with first-order accuracy. The numerical solution exhibits geometric ergodicity.
AI.conclusion: The proposed AVF method is effective for simulating GLEs with superquadratically growing potentials and degenerate noise, preserving key structural properties while achieving first-order convergence and geometric ergodicity.

title: $\texttt{cuSkyrmion}$: A CUDA-OpenGL framework for interactive simulation and visualization of nuclei as Skyrmions
summary: We introduce $\texttt{cuSkyrmion}$, a 3-dimensional Skyrme model computation and visualization software, that is written in CUDA C for rapid computation and visualization of especially the arrested Newton flow algorithm. The programme is interactive and lets the user construct Skyrmions either with configuration files, specifying coordinates, or simply in run-time using the keyboard and mouse. Rational map ansatz constituent Skyrmions can be inserted at any time and a random generator can produce a stochastic initial configuration. The software is composed into three main modules being a computational module, a rendering module and a main programme. The rendering/visualization module can readily be used by other computational modules and a Python-fork has been developed demonstrating the re-usability of the code.

AI.tldr: cuSkyrmion is a CUDA C-based interactive software for fast 3D Skyrme model computation and visualization, supporting multiple input methods and modular design with reusable rendering components.
AI.motivation: To provide a rapid, interactive platform for computing and visualizing 3D Skyrmion configurations, particularly for the arrested Newton flow algorithm, with flexibility in user input and code reusability.
AI.method: The software is written in CUDA C, structured into three modules: computation, rendering/visualization, and a main program. It supports config files, real-time keyboard/mouse input, rational map ansatz insertion, and random initial configurations. A Python fork was developed to demonstrate code reusability.
AI.result: The software enables rapid computation and visualization, with demonstrated reusability through a Python fork. It supports interactive Skyrmion construction via multiple input methods.
AI.conclusion: cuSkyrmion offers an efficient, modular, and reusable solution for 3D Skyrme model computation and visualization, enhancing user interaction and computational speed.

title: Computational homogenization of unsteady flows in a periodic porous medium
summary: The work is devoted to the development and computational implementation of the homogenization method for modeling unsteady flows of a viscous incompressible fluid in periodic porous media taking into account memory effects. At the macrolevel, the flow is described by an integro-differential Darcy law with a tensor memory kernel determined by solving unsteady problems on the periodicity cell. The developed approach to computational homogenization is based on finding the steady-state and unsteady components of the conductivity tensor from solving auxiliary boundary value and spectral problems on the periodicity cell. The nonlocal macroscopic problem is transformed into a local system of differential equations by approximating the memory kernel as a sum of exponentials. Issues of spatial finite element approximation are discussed, and stable two-level schemes in time are constructed. The results of applying the developed computational homogenization technology for unsteady filtration problems in porous media to a two-dimensional test problem are presented.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a computational homogenization method for modeling unsteady viscous fluid flows in periodic porous media with memory effects, using an integro-differential Darcy law with tensor memory kernels, and demonstrates it on a 2D test problem.
AI.motivation: To model unsteady flows in porous media that exhibit memory effects, which require a more sophisticated approach than classical Darcy's law.
AI.method: Computational homogenization using steady-state and unsteady components of the conductivity tensor from auxiliary boundary value and spectral problems on the periodicity cell, with memory kernel approximated as a sum of exponentials to transform the nonlocal problem into a local system of differential equations.
AI.result: Applied to a two-dimensional test problem for unsteady filtration in porous media.
AI.conclusion: The developed homogenization approach effectively captures memory effects in unsteady porous media flows by transforming the nonlocal integro-differential problem into a computationally tractable local system.

title: Efficient boundary elements for the Smoluchowski diffusion equation
summary: The Smoluchowski diffusion equation describes diffusion in the presence of external forces. Studying the mechanical response of soft materials to linear forces, such as shear, results in a boundary value problem involving an Ornstein-Uhlenbeck operator in an exterior domain with non-constant, unbounded coefficients. In this article, we present efficient and highly accurate boundary element methods in the frequency domain, motivated by applications in soft matter physics. Our key contributions concern the accurate assembly of the Galerkin matrix, combining the approximation of the fundamental solution as a Fourier integral with the resolution of near-field singularities. Numerical experiments demonstrate the accuracy and efficiency of the proposed methods and show their relevance for the computation of rheological quantities.

AI.tldr: This paper presents efficient and highly accurate boundary element methods for solving the Smoluchowski diffusion equation in the frequency domain, applied to soft matter physics. The key contributions are accurate assembly of the Galerkin matrix via Fourier integral approximation and near-field singularity resolution.
AI.motivation: To develop efficient and highly accurate numerical methods for studying the mechanical response of soft materials to linear forces (e.g., shear), which involves solving a boundary value problem with an Ornstein-Uhlenbeck operator in an exterior domain with non-constant, unbounded coefficients.
AI.method: Boundary element methods in the frequency domain, with accurate Galerkin matrix assembly combining Fourier integral approximation of the fundamental solution and resolution of near-field singularities.
AI.result: Numerical experiments demonstrate accuracy and efficiency of the proposed methods, showing relevance for computing rheological quantities.
AI.conclusion: The proposed boundary element methods are effective for computing rheological quantities in soft matter physics, combining Fourier integral techniques with singularity handling.

title: Sign Embedding Quantum Algorithms for Matrix Equations and Matrix Functions
summary: We develop a systematic sign-embedding framework of operator-output quantum algorithms for matrix equations and matrix functions. Differing from the contour-integral treatment, we start with the matrix-sign embedding route: an augmented matrix $M$ whose half-plane matrix sign compresses the target operator either as a block of $\text{sign}(M)$ or, in projector form, through $(I-\text{sign}(M))/2$; we then construct a logarithmic-sinc approximation for the half-plane sign operator and combine it with structure-aware scaled multiplexing and nodewise rebalancing of shifted inverse families. For ordinary Sylvester equations, we offer an explicit block-encoding of the target matrix solution with query complexity linear in the inverse-conditioning parameters and logarithmic in the target error tolerance, under non-normal and non-diagonalizable settings given a field-of-values (FoV) gap or strip-resolvent hypotheses. These algorithms propagate the same overlap-based normalization bookkeeping to ordinary and generalized Sylvester equations, generalized Lyapunov equations, principal square roots and inverse square roots, matrix geometric means, and continuous-time algebraic Riccati equations (CARE). These results identify matrix-sign embeddings and nodewise rebalancing as reusable design principles for structured operator-output quantum linear algebra.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a systematic sign-embedding framework for quantum algorithms that solve matrix equations and compute matrix functions, using logarithmic-sinc approximations and structured multiplexing to achieve query complexity linear in inverse-conditioning parameters and logarithmic in error tolerance.
AI.motivation: To create reusable, systematic quantum algorithms for solving matrix equations (Sylvester, Lyapunov, Riccati) and computing matrix functions (square roots, geometric means) that go beyond contour-integral approaches and handle non-normal, non-diagonalizable matrices.
AI.method: Uses matrix-sign embedding (augmented matrix M whose sign compresses the target operator), logarithmic-sinc approximation for the half-plane sign operator, structure-aware scaled multiplexing, and nodewise rebalancing of shifted inverse families.
AI.result: Achieves query complexity linear in inverse-conditioning parameters and logarithmic in error tolerance for Sylvester equations under FoV gap or strip-resolvent conditions. The framework extends to generalized Sylvester/Lyapunov equations, principal square roots, inverse square roots, matrix geometric means, and CARE.
AI.conclusion: Matrix-sign embeddings and nodewise rebalancing are reusable design principles for structured operator-output quantum linear algebra.

title: From Cursed to Competitive: Closing the ZO-FO Gap via Input-to-State Stability
summary: While it is generally understood that zeroth-order (ZO) algorithms have an extra dependency on their number of iterations for any choice of parameters, compared to their first-order (FO) counterparts, in this work, we show that under several conditions, in expectation, ZO methods do not suffer from extra dimension dependencies in their convergence rates with respect to their FO counterparts. We look at optimisation algorithms from the dynamical systems perspective and analyse the conditions under which one can formulate the average of a ZO algorithm as the average of its FO counterpart with bounded perturbations with values dependent on design parameters. Then, using input-to-state stability properties, we show ZO methods follow the same decay rate as their FO counterparts and converge to a neighbourhood of the fixed point of FO methods, where its radius depends on the bound of the norm of the perturbations, which can be made arbitrarily small. The theoretical findings are illustrated via numerical examples.

AI.tldr: Zeroth-order (ZO) optimization methods can achieve the same convergence rate as first-order (FO) methods in expectation, without extra dimension dependencies, under certain conditions—they converge to a neighborhood of FO fixed points where neighborhood size is controllable via design parameters.
AI.motivation: To show that ZO methods do not inherently suffer from additional dimension-dependent convergence penalties compared to FO methods, contradicting common understanding.
AI.method: Uses a dynamical systems perspective to formulate ZO algorithm averages as FO algorithm averages plus bounded perturbations. Applies input-to-state stability (ISS) properties to prove ZO methods decay at the same rate as FO methods.
AI.result: ZO methods match FO convergence rates in expectation and converge to a neighborhood of FO fixed points; the neighborhood radius depends on perturbation bounds and can be made arbitrarily small via parameter tuning.
AI.conclusion: Under specific conditions, ZO methods do not suffer extra dimension dependencies and perform comparably to FO methods with controllable approximation error.

title: Minimum-enstrophy solutions in topographic quasi-geostrophic flow on the rotating sphere
summary: The minimum-enstrophy theory of Bretherton and Haidvogel postulates that two-dimensional turbulent systems evolve to a state that minimises enstrophy at a fixed energy level.
